Glossary/Networking/Switching/ConceptsAn.bodyMd:
# Glossary - Switching - L2 Concepts & Architecture
## Layer 2 Concepts
### MAC Address
- A unique hardware identifier assigned to a device’s Network Interface Card (NIC). - Used by switches to learn device locations and make forwarding decisions. ### Frame
- The Layer 2 data unit used on Ethernet networks. Contains: - Source MAC - Destination MAC - Payload - Error-checking fields (for example FCS) ### Collision Domain
- A network segment where simultaneous transmissions can collide. In modern switched Ethernet: - Each switch port is typically its own collision domain.
## Switches and Internal Architecture
### Switch
- A Layer 2 device that forwards frames using MAC address learning. Core behavior includes: - Learning source MAC addresses per port - Building a MAC address table - Forwarding frames based on destination MAC lookup - Flooding unknown and broadcast frames Switches reduce collisions by segmenting traffic per port. ### Switch Fabric
- The internal switching architecture that enables simultaneous forwarding between ports. - Determines overall throughput and performance capabilities. ---

Glossary/Networking/Switching/HostsAndPo.title: HostsAndPorts
Glossary/Networking/Switching/HostsAndPo.bodyMd:
# Glossary - Switching - Hosts & Ports
## Hosts and Devices
### Host
- Any device connected to a network that sends or receives data. - Examples: - PCs - Macs - Smartphones - IoT devices ### Patch Cable
- A short Ethernet cable used to connect: - A switch to a router - A host (e.g., PC) to a switch - Common categories: - Cat5e - Cat6 - Cat6a
## Ports and Connections
### Router Port Naming
- Router interfaces commonly follow a format like: - GigabitEthernet0/0 - The name typically reflects: - Interface type - Speed class - Slot / module / port position (vendor-dependent) ### Ethernet Ports
- A general term for ports that use Ethernet standards for Layer 2 connectivity. ### LAN Ports
- Ports intended for connecting devices within a Local Area Network. - Often used as a label on consumer routers to distinguish internal ports from the WAN/uplink port. ### RJ-45 Ports
- Refers to the physical connector type commonly used for twisted-pair Ethernet. - Commonly supports: - Cat5e, Cat6, Cat6a ### SFP (Small Form-factor Pluggable)
- A port type and/or pluggable transceiver system used for flexible media choices. - Common media options: - Fiber optic modules - Copper modules (including RJ45-based modules, depending on platform) #### Types: - SFP - commonly up to 1 Gbps - SFP+ - commonly up to 10 Gbps ### LC Connector
- LC (Lucent Connector) - Small form-factor fiber connector commonly used in high-density environments. ### SC Connector
- SC (Subscriber/Standard Connector) - Larger push-pull fiber connector commonly seen in older installs and some residential/structured cabling. ### Switch Ports
- A general term for physical interfaces on a switch. ### Access Ports
- Switch ports configured to carry traffic for a single VLAN (common end-host port behavior). - Typically used for endpoints like PCs, printers, phones, and access points (depending on design). ### Console Ports
- Used for direct device management and initial configuration. - Common connector types: - RJ45 - USB (USB-A or USB-C) ---

Networking/TCPIPModel/Basics/Introductio.title: Introduction
Networking/TCPIPModel/Basics/Introductio.bodyMd:
# Introduction
### What Is the TCP/IP Model?
A layered model helps visualize how multiple protocols work together to enable network communication. Instead of treating communication as one large process, a layered model breaks it into structured categories of responsibility. The first widely adopted layered model for internetwork communication was created in the early 1970s and became known as the internet model. The modern suite of TCP/IP protocols follows this structure. Because of this, the internet model is commonly referred to as the TCP/IP Model.
### Why Layered Models Matter
Layered models provide several important advantages:
Benefit Why It Matters
Improved protocol design Protocols at each layer have defined responsibilities and clear interfaces to the layers above and below.
Vendor interoperability Devices from different manufacturers can work together by following the same layered standards.
Independent evolution Technology improvements at one layer do not require redesigning the entire stack.
Common language Provides shared terminology to describe networking functions and capabilities.
### The Four TCP/IP Layers
The TCP/IP Model organizes communication into four categories of function:
TCP/IP Layer General Responsibility
Application Represents data to the user and handles high-level services, encoding, and dialog control.
Transport Supports end-to-end communication between devices across diverse networks.
Internet Determines logical addressing and the best path through interconnected networks.
Network Access Controls the hardware devices and media that make up the local network.
These layers describe categories of function, not specific devices. The specific protocols operating at each layer are explored in later sections.
### Where This Fits in Your Learning Path
In Foundations, you learned: - What protocols are - Why communication requires structured rules - How multiple protocols cooperate This section now introduces the structured model that organizes those protocols. The next page explains how real protocols (like Ethernet, IP, TCP, and HTTP) map into this layered structure.

Networking/TCPIPModel/Basics/ProtocolSta.title: ProtocolStack
Networking/TCPIPModel/Basics/ProtocolSta.bodyMd:
# Understanding the Protocol Stack
A protocol stack refers to multiple protocols operating together across different layers of the TCP/IP Model to deliver a complete communication. A single network message typically uses one protocol per layer. Each protocol performs a distinct responsibility. Together, they form a structured stack.
### Example: Ethernet → IP → TCP → HTTP
Consider a simple web request. It may involve: - Network Access: Ethernet (or Wi-Fi) - Internet: IP (IPv4 or IPv6) - Transport: TCP - Application: HTTP Each protocol contributes something specific:
Layer What It Contributes
Application Defines the service being requested (e.g., retrieving a webpage).
Transport Ensures end-to-end communication behavior between source and destination.
Internet Handles logical addressing and routing across networks.
Network Access Delivers the frame across the local physical network segment.
### Local Delivery vs. Routed Delivery
When you hear: “Ethernet governs NIC-to-NIC communication” This refers to communication on the same local network segment. - Ethernet and Wi-Fi operate at the Network Access layer. - They move frames between devices on the same link. - They do not determine routing across multiple networks. When traffic must cross network boundaries: - The Internet layer (IP) determines addressing and routing. - Routers examine IP information to forward traffic appropriately.
### Layer Interaction
A layered model depicts: - Protocol operations within each layer - Interaction between adjacent layers - Defined interfaces above and below each layer Each layer depends on the services of the layer beneath it while providing services to the layer above it. This structured interaction is what allows complex communication to function reliably at scale.

Glossary/Networking/Foundations/Routers/.title: GatewayAndDefaultGateway
Glossary/Networking/Foundations/Routers/.bodyMd:
# Glossary - Foundations - Routers - Gateways & Default Gateway
### Gateway
A device, typically a router, that provides an exit path for traffic to leave one local network and be forwarded to remote networks or the Internet. The gateway acts as the boundary separating local and remote networks.
### Default Gateway
The IPv4 address of the router interface directly connected to the local network, serving as the exit point ("door out of the room") for any traffic destined for a remote network. Hosts use this address when the destination IP is not on the local subnet.
  • Determined by binary ANDing the source IP with the subnet mask and comparing it to the destination IP AND subnet mask.
  • If network portions do not match, the host sends the packet to the default gateway.
  • The host then ARPs for the MAC address of the default gateway to encapsulate the frame.
  • Can be configured statically or received dynamically via DHCP (common on wireless routers).
  • Misconfiguration (e.g., wrong IP or different subnet) prevents the host from reaching the gateway via ARP.
Required on hosts for communication beyond the local network (e.g., Internet access).
### Router Interface (as Gateway)
The router port (physical or logical) connected to a specific local network; its assigned IPv4 address becomes the default gateway for all hosts on that directly connected network. Each interface separates one network from others and defines a boundary.
  • The interface IP is typically the first usable address in the subnet (e.g., 192.168.1.1).
  • Hosts on the connected LAN must use addresses in the same subnet as the interface.
### Internal (Inside) Network
The local/private side of a router (e.g., the LAN side of a home/SOHO wireless router), where hosts use private IPv4 addresses (RFC 1918 ranges). These addresses are not directly routable on the public Internet.
### External (Outside) Network
The WAN/Internet side of a router, where the router typically receives a public/routable IPv4 address from the ISP (often via DHCP client on the WAN interface). The router forms the boundary between the internal private network and the external public Internet.

# Glossary - Network Access - MAC Address
### MAC Address
48-bit physical address assigned to NICs for Layer 2 communication on local networks.
### Local vs Remote MAC Usage
Local: Destination MAC = target device. Remote: Destination MAC = default gateway. MAC changes hop-by-hop; IP remains constant.
See: Data Link - Frames & Addressing - MAC Addressing

Glossary/Networking/TCPIPModel/Internet/.title: Basics
Glossary/Networking/TCPIPModel/Internet/.bodyMd:
# Glossary - Internet - Basics

<hr class="dividerSection" />

### IP Address

<hr class="dividerSection" />

Logical identifier assigned to devices on a network to enable communication (Layer 3 of the TCP/IP model).

### Broadcast Domain

<hr class="dividerSection" />

Logical network segment where all devices receive broadcast frames sent by any device in that domain.

Switches flood broadcasts; routers do not forward them.

### Default Gateway

<hr class="dividerSection" />

The IP address of the router interface that hosts use to reach remote networks or the Internet.

### Regional Internet Registry (RIR)

<hr class="dividerSection" />

One of five organizations that allocate public IPv4 and IPv6 address blocks from IANA to ISPs and organizations.

The five RIRs are ARIN, RIPE NCC, APNIC, AfriNIC, and LACNIC.

<hr class="dividerSection" />

### DHCP Discover Packet

<hr class="dividerSection" />

Broadcast message sent by a client to locate any available DHCP server on the network.

Includes the client's MAC address.

### DHCP Offer Packet

<hr class="dividerSection" />

Response from a DHCP server to the client's Discover message, offering an available IP address, subnet mask, default gateway, and other configuration details.

### DHCP Request Packet

<hr class="dividerSection" />

Message sent by the client to accept the offered IP address from the DHCP server.

### DHCP Acknowledge (ACK) Packet

<hr class="dividerSection" />

Final confirmation from the DHCP server to the client, assigning the IP address and updating its table with the client's MAC address.

<hr class="dividerSection" />

Glossary/Networking/TCPIPModel/Internet/.title: IPv4Addressing
Glossary/Networking/TCPIPModel/Internet/.bodyMd:
# Glossary - Internet - IPv4 Addressing
### IPv4 (Internet Protocol version 4)
32-bit logical address written as four decimal octets separated by dots (e.g., 192.168.1.10).
### Octets
Four 8-bit groups (0–255 each) that make up an IPv4 address.
### Dotted-Decimal Notation
Human-readable format for IPv4 addresses (four decimal numbers separated by dots).
### Hierarchical Addressing
IPv4 addresses have a network portion (identifies the network) and host portion (identifies the device). Routers only track networks, not individual hosts. Analogy: Telephone system — country/area/exchange code = network, remaining digits = host.
### Network Portion
Part of the IP address that identifies the network (defined by subnet mask/CIDR).
### Host Portion
Part of the IP address that identifies the specific device on that network.
### Subnet
Smaller network created by dividing a larger network using subnet masks or CIDR notation. Reduces broadcast traffic and improves performance/security.
### Subnet Mask
32-bit value that separates network and host portions (e.g., 255.255.255.0).
### CIDR Notation
Shorthand for subnet masks (e.g., /24 = 255.255.255.0).
### Subnetting
Process of dividing a network into smaller subnetworks by borrowing bits from the host portion.
### Unicast
One-to-one communication between a single source and single destination. Source IP address is always unicast (packet originates from one device). Used for normal host-to-host traffic (web browsing, email, file transfer).
### Broadcast
One-to-all communication on the local network.
  • Directed broadcast: to all hosts on a specific network (e.g., 192.168.1.255)
  • Limited broadcast: to all hosts on local LAN (255.255.255.255)
Routers do not forward broadcast packets. Used for discovery (e.g., ARP requests).
### Multicast
One-to-many communication for a group of devices that subscribe to a multicast group. IPv4 range: 224.0.0.0–239.255.255.255 Reduces traffic (one packet instead of multiple unicast copies). Common use: routing protocols (e.g., OSPF uses 224.0.0.5). Only subscribed devices process the packet.
### Public IPv4 Addresses
Globally unique addresses that are routable across the public Internet. Allocated by regional Internet registries and used for devices that must be directly reachable from the Internet.
### Private IPv4 Addresses
Addresses reserved for internal networks (defined in RFC 1918) that are not routable on the public Internet. Common ranges:
  • 10.0.0.0 – 10.255.255.255 (/8)
  • 172.16.0.0 – 172.31.255.255 (/12)
  • 192.168.0.0 – 192.168.255.255 (/16)
Used widely in home and enterprise networks, often with NAT to access the Internet.
### Network Address Translation (NAT)
Technique that translates private IPv4 addresses to public IPv4 addresses for Internet access. Allows multiple internal devices to share one or more public addresses for outbound traffic and return replies to the correct internal device. Detailed NAT concepts are covered in Advanced → NAT & PAT.
See Advanced for full details: Advanced → NAT & PAT → Concepts & Types

Glossary/Networking/TCPIPModel/Internet/.title: IPv6Addressing
Glossary/Networking/TCPIPModel/Internet/.bodyMd:
# Glossary - Internet - IPv6 Addressing

<hr class="dividerSection" />

### IPv6

<hr class="dividerSection" />

128-bit logical address written in hexadecimal, with groups separated by colons (e.g., 2001:0db8::1).

Designed to solve IPv4 address exhaustion with a vastly larger address space (340 undecillion addresses).

<hr class="dividerSubsection1" />

### Hexadecimal Notation

<hr class="dividerSubsection1" />

The standard human-readable format for IPv6 addresses, using eight groups of four hexadecimal digits separated by colons.

Computers process IPv6 addresses in their 128-bit binary form, but hexadecimal notation provides a compact and readable representation for human use.

### Internet of Things (IoT)

<hr class="dividerSection" />

The Internet of Things (IoT) refers to the network of sensor-equipped, internet-ready devices beyond traditional computers (e.g., automobiles, biomedical devices, household appliances, natural ecosystems).

IPv6 is designed to support the massive scale of IoT with its enormous address space.

<hr class="dividerSection" />

Glossary/Networking/TCPIPModel/Internet/.title: NATAndPAT
Glossary/Networking/TCPIPModel/Internet/.bodyMd:
# Glossary - Internet - NAT & PAT
### Network Address Translation (NAT)
Process that allows devices on a private network to use private IPv4 addresses while communicating with devices on the public Internet by translating those addresses to a public IPv4 address. Primarily solves IPv4 address exhaustion (RFC 1918 private ranges) and hides internal network structure for basic security.
### Port Address Translation (PAT)
A form of NAT (also called NAT overload) where multiple private IPv4 addresses are mapped to a single public IPv4 address using unique port numbers. Most common type used in small/home/SOHO networks; allows many internal devices to share one public address for outbound Internet access.
### Inside Local Address
The private IPv4 address assigned to a host on the internal (inside) network before any NAT translation occurs. Not routable on the public Internet.
### Inside Global Address
The public IPv4 address that represents one or more inside local addresses to devices on the outside (Internet) network after NAT translation. This is the address visible externally.
### Outside Local Address
The address of an outside host as it appears to the inside network (usually the same as the outside global address unless destination NAT is involved). In basic NAT/PAT setups, this is typically unchanged.
### Outside Global Address
The public IPv4 address of a host on the outside network (Internet) as seen from anywhere. Routable on the public Internet.
### Static NAT
One-to-one permanent mapping between a private (inside local) address and a public (inside global) address. Used for servers that need consistent external access; less common in small networks.
### Dynamic NAT
Temporary one-to-one mapping from a pool of public addresses to private addresses. Requires enough public addresses for simultaneous connections; rarely used in small networks due to address scarcity.
### NAT Overload
Synonym for PAT; uses port numbers to differentiate multiple internal sessions sharing one public address. Most efficient for small networks with limited public addresses.
For full concepts, operation details, small-network examples, and use cases/limits, see:
TCP/IP Model → Internet Layer → Advanced → NAT & PAT → Concepts & Types
TCP/IP Model → Internet Layer → Advanced → NAT & PAT → Use Cases & Limits

Networking/Foundations/Basics/Fundamenta.title: CommunicationProtocols
Networking/Foundations/Basics/Fundamenta.bodyMd:
# Introduction --- ### What Are Communication Protocols?
A protocol is a set of rules that define how communication happens. In human communication, we follow “protocols” without consciously thinking about them. Communication often happens in layers: - We choose a shared **language** - We follow agreed rules of **grammar and structure** - We select a **delivery method** (in person, phone, email) - We follow expected **timing** - We may confirm receipt (“Got it”, a reply, or acknowledgment) Each rule handles a different part of communication. Together, they allow the full message to be understood. Network communication works the same way — devices follow agreed-upon rules so data can be delivered and interpreted correctly.
See: Foundations → Basics → Fundamentals → Connected Devices
### Protocol Requirements for Successful Communication
Whether it’s people or devices, successful communication usually requires:
Requirement What It Means
Identified sender and receiver Both sides must be known so messages reach the correct destination.
Agreed method of communication The medium must be compatible (text, call, email; Wi-Fi, Ethernet, etc.).
Common language and grammar Both sides must interpret the message format the same way.
Speed and timing rules Defines delivery timing, retries, and pacing expectations.
Confirmation requirements Some communications require acknowledgment or retransmission.
A simple everyday example is sending a text message: - You expect it to arrive in readable form. - You expect it within a reasonable time. - You may resend it if it fails. - You may rely on “delivered” or “read” indicators. Networks implement similar expectations using structured communication protocols.
### Protocol Characteristics Devices Must Agree On
Protocols define specific characteristics so communication remains consistent and predictable.
Characteristic Why It Matters
Message format Defines the structure of the data so the receiver can parse it correctly.
Message size Defines how large messages can be and when they must be split.
Timing Defines when data is sent, how long to wait, and when to retry.
Encoding Defines how bits represent information.
Encapsulation Adds structured information so data can be delivered and validated.
Message patterns Defines communication flows (request/response, streaming, acknowledgments).
### Many Protocols Working Together
Most network communication relies on multiple protocols working together. A single action — such as loading a webpage — does not use just one protocol. Instead, several protocols cooperate, each handling a different responsibility. You can think of a web request as a simple stacked example: - **HTTP** handles the webpage request and response. - **TCP** helps ensure the data arrives reliably and in order. - **IP** determines how packets travel from source to destination across networks. - **Ethernet or Wi-Fi** delivers the data across the local network connection. Each protocol focuses on one task. Together, they provide complete end-to-end communication. This layered cooperation is foundational to how modern networks operate.
### How Devices “See” the Network
Humans can visualize a network using diagrams showing: - End devices (PCs, servers) - Intermediate devices (switches, routers) - Addressing information (MAC address, IP address, default gateway) Devices, however, do not “see” diagrams. You can think of each device as operating within its own bubble: - It knows its own configuration. - It does not automatically know the full network layout. - It relies on protocols to discover and reach other devices. Protocols answer questions such as: - What is my address? - Is the destination local or remote? - Where should I send data next? - How do I resolve names into addresses? - How do I confirm successful delivery?
### Why Protocols Matter Before Infrastructure Details
Before diving into switches, routers, and cabling, understand this key distinction: - Infrastructure provides physical connectivity. - Protocols provide the rules that make communication possible. A network can have perfectly functioning hardware, but communication will still fail if devices are not using compatible protocols. You will explore how these protocols are organized in a structured model in the upcoming TCP/IP and OSI sections.
Topic: Foundations → Basics → Devices & Models → Network Infrastructure

Networking/Foundations/Basics/Fundamenta.title: ConnectedDevices
Networking/Foundations/Basics/Fundamenta.bodyMd:
# Introduction --- ### What Are Connected Devices? --- A connected device is any device that communicates on a network using wired or wireless connectivity. In a home or small office environment, most connected devices are considered end hosts because they generate or consume data rather than forwarding traffic for other devices. These devices connect to the LAN (Local Area Network) side of a router or switch.
See: Foundations → Devices & Models → Home Network Architecture
--- ### Common Types of Connected Devices --- Home and small office networks commonly include: - Desktop computers - Laptops - Gaming consoles - Smart TVs - Printers and scanners - Security cameras - Smart speakers - Telephones (VoIP or Wi-Fi calling) - Climate control systems (smart thermostats) - Mobile devices (smartphones and tablets) As new technologies emerge, more household functions rely on network connectivity for monitoring, automation, and remote control. --- ### Wired vs Wireless Devices --- Connected devices may attach to the network in different ways:
Connection Type Description
Wired (Ethernet) Connected using an RJ45 Ethernet cable to a LAN port on a router or switch.
Wireless (Wi-Fi) Connected through the router’s built-in wireless access point.
In most home networks, wired and wireless devices share the same LAN and can communicate with each other directly.
See: Foundations → Devices & Models → Routers → Port Types
--- ### Infrastructure vs End Devices --- Not all devices on a network serve the same purpose.
Device Category Purpose
End Devices Generate and consume data (PCs, TVs, phones, IoT devices).
Infrastructure Devices Forward, manage, or secure traffic (routers, switches, access points).
Infrastructure devices connect networks together, while end devices primarily use network services. --- ### Growth of IoT in Home Networks --- Modern networks increasingly support Internet of Things (IoT) devices. Examples include: - Smart lighting systems - Smart locks - Video doorbells - Environmental sensors - Voice assistants The growth of IoT increases: - The number of connected devices - Bandwidth demand - Security considerations As networks expand, proper segmentation and secure configuration become more important. --- ### Relationship to WLAN --- In home environments, many devices connect wirelessly using a Wireless Local Area Network (WLAN). Wireless devices: - Associate with a wireless access point - Receive an IP address from the router - Join the same local network as wired devices (by default)
See: Wireless → WLAN Architecture → BSS/ESS/SSID
---

Networking/Foundations/Basics/Fundamenta.title: Internet
Networking/Foundations/Basics/Fundamenta.bodyMd:
# Who Owns the Internet? --- The **internet is not owned by any single individual or organization.** It is a **global collection of interconnected networks** that follow standardized communication protocols. - It operates as a **"network of networks,"** where multiple smaller networks interconnect. - **Data travels** through various technologies, including **fiber-optic cables, wireless transmission, and satellites**. --- ## **Who Regulates the Internet?** --- Although no single entity owns the internet, **organizations help maintain its structure**: - **ICANN:** Manages domain names and IP addresses. - **IETF:** Develops and maintains internet communication standards. - **IANA & Regional Internet Registries (RIRs):** Allocate IP addresses to ISPs.

--- ## **How the Internet Works** --- The internet allows **devices and networks** to exchange data efficiently. 1. **Internet Service Providers (ISPs)** provide access to homes and businesses. 2. **Packets of data** travel across interconnected networks, passing through multiple routing points. 3. **Websites, cloud applications, and databases** are hosted on **remote servers**. ### **Example: How a Webpage Loads** 1. A user enters www.example.com in a web browser. 2. The browser contacts a **DNS server** to retrieve the **IP address** of the website. 3. The request is routed across the **internet backbone** to the web server. 4. The web server responds, and the page is displayed. *(For details on DNS, refer to the **DNS and Name Resolution** section.)* --- ## **The Role of ISPs and the Internet Backbone** --- An Internet Service Provider (ISP) connects home and business networks to the global internet. ISPs may be: - Cable providers - Telephone companies - Cellular providers - Independent providers leasing infrastructure ISPs interconnect with other ISPs in a hierarchical structure to form the global internet. At the highest level, major providers are connected through the internet backbone — a high-capacity network of fiber-optic links that connect major metropolitan areas worldwide. Fiber-optic cables: - Run underground across continents - Run under oceans between continents - Provide extremely high bandwidth and reliability --- ## Internet Connection Types --- Internet connections vary in **speed, reliability, and infrastructure**.
Connection Type Speed
Fiber Optic 1 Gbps or more
DSL 1–100 Mbps
Satellite Up to 100 Mbps
5G Wireless Up to 10 Gbps

Connection Type Common Usage
Fiber Optic High-speed residential & business
DSL Home broadband
Satellite Rural areas
5G Wireless Mobile internet

--- ### Geographic Availability & Infrastructure --- Most home network users do not connect directly to their service providers with fiber-optic cables. The type of internet connection available depends largely on: - geographic location - existing infrastructure - service provider availability In metropolitan areas, many apartments and offices are increasingly connected directly with fiber-optic cables, enabling very high bandwidth and support for bundled services such as internet, phone, and television. In suburban, small-town, and rural areas, other connection methods are more common. --- ### Cable Internet --- Cable internet is typically offered by cable television service providers. - The internet data signal is carried on the same coaxial cable that delivers cable television. - It provides a high-bandwidth, always-on connection. - A special cable modem separates the internet data signal from other signals on the cable. - The modem provides an Ethernet connection to a computer or local network (LAN). Cable is widely available in suburban and urban areas.
Physical Media Details: TCP/IP Model → Network Access → Physical → Cabling & Interfaces → Coaxial Cable
--- ### DSL (Digital Subscriber Line) --- DSL provides a high-bandwidth, always-on connection using traditional telephone lines. - Requires a special DSL modem. - The modem separates the DSL data signal from the telephone voice signal. - Provides an Ethernet connection to a host computer or LAN. DSL divides the telephone line into three channels: 1. A voice channel for telephone calls. 2. A faster download channel for receiving data. 3. An upload channel for sending data (usually slower than download). Users can make phone calls without disconnecting from the internet. The quality and speed of a DSL connection depend primarily on: - the quality of the telephone line - the distance from the telephone company’s central office The farther a location is from the central office, the slower the DSL connection. DSL is common in small towns and some rural areas, but may not be available in very remote regions. --- ### Cellular Internet --- Cellular internet access uses a mobile carrier network (such as LTE or 5G) to provide connectivity. Wherever a cellular signal is available, internet access may be possible. Performance depends on: - signal strength - tower congestion - device capabilities - service plan limitations Carriers often meter data usage and may charge additional fees or throttle speeds after certain limits. --- ### Mobile Hotspots (Tethering) --- Many smartphones and dedicated mobile devices can operate as a mobile hotspot. A hotspot: - Uses the cellular network as its wide area (WAN) connection. - Shares that connection locally so nearby devices can access the internet (typically over Wi-Fi). The term “hotspot” refers to the localized wireless access area created by the device — not device temperature.
For deeper detail, see: Mobile → Basics → Tethering → Hotspots & Tethering
--- ### Satellite Internet --- Satellite service is a good option for homes or offices that do not have access to DSL or cable. - Requires a satellite dish with a clear line-of-sight to the satellite. - Can be difficult in heavily wooded areas or locations with physical obstructions. - Equipment and installation costs may be higher than other options. - Monthly service plans vary by provider and speed tier. Satellite internet is particularly valuable in remote or rural areas where no other broadband infrastructure exists. Because signals must travel long distances between Earth and orbiting satellites, satellite connections typically have higher latency than cable or DSL. Severe weather (heavy rain or snow) may temporarily degrade signal quality. --- ### Dial-Up Internet (Legacy) --- Dial-up is an older and inexpensive connection method that uses a traditional telephone line and modem. - The modem calls the ISP’s access phone number to establish a connection. - Provides very low bandwidth compared to broadband options. - Typically prevents phone calls while connected. Dial-up should only be considered when higher-speed connections are not available. It has largely been replaced by modern broadband technologies. --- ### **For More on Speed Evolution** ---
See: TCP/IP Model → Network Access → Physical → Bandwidth
--- ### **Future of the Internet** --- The internet continues to evolve with new technologies: - **IPv6 Expansion:** Enables more devices to connect. - **Low Earth Orbit (LEO) Satellites:** Expand global internet coverage. - **Edge Computing:** Improves speed and reduces latency. ---

Networking/Foundations/Basics/Fundamenta.title: Introduction
Networking/Foundations/Basics/Fundamenta.bodyMd:
--- ## What is Networking? --- Networking is the process of **connecting devices** to share data and resources. It enables **communication and collaboration** between users and systems across small or large distances. Devices in a network take on one of two main roles:
- Client: Requests services or data. - Server: Provides services or data.
**Real-World Examples:**
- A **printer** on a home network allows multiple devices to send print jobs wirelessly. - A **company network** enables employees to share files and access cloud-based applications. - **Online gaming servers** connect players from around the world.
--- ## Network Types Overview --- Different network types are used depending on the **size, coverage area, and purpose** of the network.
Network Type Coverage Area
LAN (Local Area Network) Within a home, school, or office
WAN (Wide Area Network) Covers large geographical areas
MAN (Metropolitan Area Network) Covers a city or large campus

Network Type Example Use
LAN (Local Area Network) Connecting computers in a school
WAN (Wide Area Network) The internet, a corporate global network
MAN (Metropolitan Area Network) University networks, municipal networks

--- ## The Client-Server Model --- 1. Clients: Initiate communication by making **requests** (e.g., opening a **webpage** or accessing a **shared file**). 2. Servers: Respond to these **requests**, providing data or resources. **Real-World Example:** - A **web browser (client)** requests a webpage from a **web server**. - A **company laptop** accesses a shared document from a **file server**. ---

Networking/Foundations/Basics/Fundamenta.title: LocalNetworks
Networking/Foundations/Basics/Fundamenta.bodyMd:
# What Are Local Networks?
A local network is a private network that connects devices within a limited area, such as a home, office, or campus.
  • Devices within a local network can communicate without using the public internet.
  • These networks allow file sharing, resource sharing, and communication between connected systems.
## Types of Local Networks
Local networks vary in size, purpose, and infrastructure.
Network Type Common Use Case
Small Home Network Basic internet access and device sharing
SOHO (Small Office/Home Office) Network Small business operations and remote work
Enterprise Network Corporate infrastructure spanning multiple locations
Campus Network Large educational institutions
Network Type Example
Small Home Network Wi-Fi router in a house
SOHO (Small Office/Home Office) Network Home office setup with network printers
Enterprise Network Corporate HQ with data centers and VLANs
Campus Network University networks connecting departments
## Key Features of Local Networks
Local networks offer efficient communication by keeping traffic within a confined area.
  • Shared Resources: Printers, storage devices, and servers can be accessed by multiple users.
  • Internal Communication: Employees or family members can send files and messages without needing the internet.
  • Security & Control: Organizations can set up firewalls, authentication methods, and encryption to protect data.
  • Cost Efficiency: Reduces the need for expensive external cloud services.
## Enterprise vs. Small Networks
Local networks can range from a simple home setup to complex enterprise infrastructures.
Feature Small Networks
Scale Few devices, single location
Security Basic firewall and password protection
Network Management Basic router settings
Internet Access Single broadband connection
Feature Enterprise Networks
Scale Hundreds to thousands of devices, multiple locations
Security Advanced firewalls, VPNs, and intrusion detection
Network Management Managed switches, VLANs, and monitoring tools
Internet Access Redundant connections with load balancing
## Evolution of LAN Definition
The term LAN (Local Area Network) refers to a local network or group of interconnected local networks under the same administrative control.
  • In early networking, LANs were small networks in a single physical location.
  • Today, a LAN can span multiple buildings or locations with hundreds of hosts.
  • LANs typically use Ethernet or wireless protocols and support high data rates.
  • The term intranet is often used for a private LAN belonging to an organization, accessible only by authorized members.
## Local vs Remote Network Segments
Within a LAN, hosts can be placed on a single local network or divided across multiple networks connected by a distribution layer device (router).
  • Local Segment: All hosts on the same network communicate directly (using ARP for MAC addresses).
  • Remote Segment: Hosts on different networks require routing through a router.
**Advantages of a Single Local Segment:**
  • Appropriate for simpler networks
  • Less complexity and lower network cost
  • Allows devices to be "seen" by others
  • Faster data transfer (more direct communication)
  • Ease of device access
**Disadvantages of a Single Local Segment:**
  • All hosts in one broadcast domain causes more traffic and may slow performance
  • Harder to implement QoS
  • Harder to implement security
**Advantages of Hosts on Remote Segments:**
  • More appropriate for larger, more complex networks
  • Splits broadcast domains and decreases traffic
  • Can improve performance on each segment
  • Makes machines invisible to other local network segments
  • Provides increased security
  • Can improve network organization
**Disadvantages of Hosts on Remote Segments:**
  • Requires routing (distribution layer)
  • Router can introduce latency or time delay on packets between segments
  • More complexity and expense (requires a router)
See: Default Gateway & Routing Tables (local/remote determination and routing process)
## The Role of Local Networks in the Internet
While local networks operate independently, they often connect to the internet for global communication.
  • LANs (Local Area Networks) form the foundation of businesses, schools, and homes.
  • The internet itself is a network of interconnected LANs.

Networking/Foundations/Basics/Fundamenta.title: StandardsAndOrganizations
Networking/Foundations/Basics/Fundamenta.bodyMd:
# Standards & Organizations --- ## Why Standards Matter --- With the increasing number of new devices and technologies coming online, how is it possible to manage all the changes and still reliably deliver services such as email? The answer is **internet standards**. A **standard** is a set of rules that determines how something must be done. Networking and internet standards ensure that all devices connecting to the network implement the same set of rules or protocols in the same manner. Using standards, it is possible for different types of devices to send information to each other over the internet. For example, the way in which an email is formatted, forwarded, and received by all devices is done according to a standard. If one person sends an email via a personal computer, another person can use a mobile phone to receive and read the email as long as the mobile phone uses the same standards as the personal computer. --- ## How Internet Standards Are Created (RFCs) --- An internet standard is the end result of a comprehensive cycle of **discussion, problem solving, and testing**. When a new standard is proposed, each stage of the development and approval process is recorded in a numbered **Request for Comments (RFC)** document so that the evolution of the standard is tracked. RFCs for internet standards are **published and managed by the Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF)**. --- ## Common Standards Bodies (High-Level) --- Different organizations support standards across different parts of networking. Examples include: - **IETF** — publishes RFCs and standardizes many core internet protocols. - **IEEE** — defines many LAN/WLAN technical standards (ex: 802.3 Ethernet, 802.11 Wi-Fi). - **ICANN** — coordinates global domain names and IP-related resources. - **IANA** — manages core internet registries (IP allocations, protocol parameters, etc.). - **ITU** — develops global telecom standards (especially carrier / international telecom space). - **TIA** — defines many cabling and physical infrastructure standards (ex: wiring specs). > Note: You’ll see these organizations referenced again in specific sections (Ethernet, Wi-Fi, cabling, routing, etc.). > This page is the “big picture”; other pages are the “how it works in real life.” --- ## 📎 Related Topics ---
See: TCP/IP Model → Network Access → Physical → Transmission Concepts → Communication Standards
See: Wireless → 802.11 Fundamentals → Wireless Standards
---

Networking/Mobile/Basics/Bluetooth/Intro.title: Introduction
Networking/Mobile/Basics/Bluetooth/Intro.bodyMd:
# Introduction --- ### Bluetooth Introduction --- Bluetooth is a short-range wireless technology that allows devices to connect to each other and to nearby accessories. Cellular and Wi-Fi connections can be difficult to configure and often require additional infrastructure (such as towers and access points). Cable connections are not always practical when connecting headsets, speakers, or other peripherals. Bluetooth provides a simple connection method that is: - wireless - automatic - low power (helps conserve battery life) Up to eight Bluetooth devices can be connected together at one time. Bluetooth is commonly used on smartphones, tablets, laptops, and desktops for short-range accessory and device connections. --- ### Common Bluetooth Uses --- These are common examples of how devices use Bluetooth:
Example How It Uses Bluetooth
Hands-free headset A small earpiece with a microphone used for making and receiving calls.
Keyboard or mouse Connects input devices to a mobile device to make typing and navigation easier.
Stereo control Connects to a home or car stereo to play audio from a mobile device.
Car speakerphone Uses a device with a speaker and microphone for hands-free calling.
Tethering Shares a network connection with another mobile device or computer (can also be done via Wi-Fi or USB).
Mobile speaker Connects portable speakers to provide higher-quality audio without a stereo system.
---

Networking/Mobile/Basics/Bluetooth/Pairi.title: PairingAndProfiles
Networking/Mobile/Basics/Bluetooth/Pairi.bodyMd:
# Introduction --- ### Pairing & Profiles --- Bluetooth pairing occurs when two Bluetooth devices establish a connection to share resources. To pair devices: - Bluetooth radios must be turned on. - One device searches for other devices. - The other device must be set to discoverable mode (also called visible) so it can be detected. When a Bluetooth device is in discoverable mode, it can transmit information when another device requests it, such as: - Name - Bluetooth class - Services the device can use - Technical information (such as supported features or the Bluetooth specification level) --- ### PIN / Passkey Authentication --- During pairing, a PIN may be requested to authenticate the pairing process. - The PIN is often a number, but it may also be a numeric code or passkey. - Once paired, the PIN is stored so it does not have to be entered again for future connections. This is convenient for accessories such as headsets, which may automatically connect when powered on and within range. --- ### Pairing Steps (Android) --- To pair a Bluetooth device with an Android device: 1. Follow the instructions for your device to place it in discoverable mode. 2. Check the device instructions to find the connection PIN (if required). 3. Select Settings > Wireless and networks. 4. Touch Bluetooth to turn it on. 5. Touch the Bluetooth tab. 6. Touch Scan for devices. 7. Touch the discovered device to select it. 8. Type the PIN. 9. Touch the device name again to connect. --- ### Pairing Steps (iOS) --- To pair a Bluetooth device with an iOS device: 1. Follow the instructions for your device to place it in discoverable mode. 2. Check the device instructions to find the connection PIN (if required). 3. Select Settings > Bluetooth. 4. Touch Bluetooth to turn it on. 5. Touch the discovered device to select it. 6. Type the PIN. --- ### Notes on Device Differences --- Mobile device operating systems are updated frequently. Always refer to the documentation of the manufacturer for your specific model device for the latest command reference. ---

When moving between cells, the phone performs a **handoff**, maintaining active connections without interruption. --- ### Data Plans Mobile data plans determine: - Monthly data limits - Speed tiers - Throttling thresholds - Roaming policies When not connected to Wi-Fi, internet traffic uses the cellular data plan. --- Cellular networks provide wide-area connectivity but may be affected by: - Terrain - Building materials - Network congestion ---

Networking/Mobile/Basics/Cellular/Introd.title: Introduction
Networking/Mobile/Basics/Cellular/Introd.bodyMd:
Cellular networks allow mobile devices to communicate over large geographic areas. A smartphone connects to nearby cell towers operated by a mobile carrier. These towers provide: - Voice communication - Text messaging - Mobile data services Unlike Wi-Fi, cellular networks are managed by telecommunications providers. --- ### How Cellular Communication Works 1. The phone connects to the nearest cell tower. 2. The tower connects to the carrier’s core network. 3. The carrier routes traffic to: - Other phones - The public telephone network - The internet This infrastructure allows users to communicate across cities, states, and countries. --- ### Generations of Cellular Technology - 3G – Early mobile data - 4G LTE – High-speed broadband - 5G – Very high speed, low latency Each generation improves speed, reliability, and network efficiency. ---

Networking/Mobile/Basics/Fundamentals/In.title: Introduction
Networking/Mobile/Basics/Fundamentals/In.bodyMd:
# Introduction --- ## Mobile Devices & Wireless Communication --- Mobile devices give us the freedom to work, learn, play, and communicate from almost anywhere. People using mobile devices do not need to be tied to a physical location to send and receive voice, video, and data communications. Wireless facilities (such as internet cafés) and wireless networks on college campuses allow users to: - sign up for classes - watch lectures - submit assignments - complete tasks in areas where physical network connections are unavailable With mobile devices becoming more powerful, many tasks that once required large computers connected to physical networks can now be completed using mobile devices on wireless networks. --- ## Multiple Radios, Multiple Networks --- Modern smartphones contain multiple built-in radios that allow them to connect to different types of networks. Unlike a laptop that primarily connects using Wi-Fi, a smartphone is designed to communicate using several wireless technologies at the same time. These radios enable the phone to: - Make voice calls - Access the internet using a data plan - Connect to Wi-Fi networks - Pair with accessories - Determine location - Make contactless payments Each function uses a different wireless technology. --- ## Radios Inside a Smartphone --- A typical smartphone includes: - **Cellular radio** – Connects to mobile carrier networks (4G / 5G) - **Wi-Fi radio** – Connects to local wireless networks (WLAN) - **Bluetooth radio** – Connects to nearby accessories - **NFC radio** – Enables short-range communication for payments - **GPS receiver** – Determines geographic location using satellites These radios operate independently but may function simultaneously. --- ## Wi-Fi vs Cellular Data --- Almost all mobile devices are capable of connecting to Wi-Fi networks. It is advisable to connect to Wi-Fi when possible because: - data used over Wi-Fi does **not** count against the cellular data plan - Wi-Fi radios usually use **less power** than cellular radios, which helps conserve battery life --- ## Wi-Fi Security on Mobile Devices --- Like other Wi-Fi-enabled devices, it is important to use security when connecting to Wi-Fi networks. Precautions to protect Wi-Fi communications include: - Never send login or password information using unencrypted text (plaintext). - Use a VPN connection when possible if you are sending sensitive data. - Enable security on home networks. - Use WPA2 or higher encryption for security. --- ## Why Multiple Radios? --- Different wireless technologies are optimized for different purposes: - Long-range wide-area coverage → Cellular - Local internet access → Wi-Fi - Short-range device pairing → Bluetooth - Very short-range secure exchange → NFC - Global positioning → GPS Understanding which radio is used for each service helps explain how mobile devices communicate. ---

Networking/Mobile/Basics/Fundamentals/Ne.title: NetworkSelection
Networking/Mobile/Basics/Fundamentals/Ne.bodyMd:
# Introduction --- ### Network Selection & Wi-Fi Connection Behavior --- Two of the most popular operating systems for mobile devices are **Android** and **Apple iOS**. Each operating system has settings that enable you to configure your device to connect to wireless networks. When an Android or iOS device is within the coverage range of a Wi-Fi network: - Turn on Wi-Fi. - The device searches for available Wi-Fi networks and displays them in a list. - Touch a Wi-Fi network in the list to connect. - Enter a password if needed. When a mobile device is out of range of the Wi-Fi network: - It attempts to connect to another Wi-Fi network in range. - If no Wi-Fi networks are available, it connects to the cellular data network. When Wi-Fi is on, the device automatically reconnects to any Wi-Fi network it has connected to previously. If the network is new, the device may display a list of available networks or ask if it should connect. --- ### SSID and Passphrase Basics --- A mobile device connects to a Wi-Fi network using two main pieces of information: - SSID (the wireless network name) - Passphrase (the wireless password) Both must be entered exactly as configured on the wireless router. If they are entered incorrectly, the device will not connect properly. If a mobile device does not prompt to connect to a Wi-Fi network: - the SSID broadcast may be turned off - the device may not be set to connect automatically In these cases, you may need to manually configure the Wi-Fi settings on the device.
See: Wireless → WLAN Architecture → BSS/ESS/SSID
--- ### Manual Connection (Android) --- To connect to a Wi-Fi network manually on an Android device: 1. Select **Settings > Add network**. 2. Enter the network SSID. 3. Touch **Security** and select a security type. 4. Touch **Password** and enter the password. 5. Touch **Save**. --- ### Manual Connection (iOS) --- To connect to a Wi-Fi network manually on an iOS device: 1. Select **Settings > Wi-Fi > Other**. 2. Enter the network SSID. 3. Touch **Security** and select a security type. 4. Touch **Other Network**. 5. Touch **Password** and enter the password. 6. Touch **Join**. --- ### Notes on Device Differences --- Operating systems for mobile devices are updated frequently and may be customized by the device manufacturer. The commands listed above may not be exactly the same on your device. Online manuals for every type of device are usually available from the manufacturer’s website. ---

Networking/RoutingProtocols/Basics/Funda.title: StaticVsDynamic
Networking/RoutingProtocols/Basics/Funda.bodyMd:
# Static vs Dynamic Routing
Routing is the process of moving packets between networks. Routers build and maintain routing tables in two main ways:
  • Static Routing — Routes are manually entered by the administrator (simple but not scalable).
  • Dynamic Routing — Routes are learned and updated automatically using routing protocols (scalable for large networks).
**Static Routing advantages**:
  • Simple and predictable
  • No protocol overhead
  • Secure (no route advertisements)
**Dynamic Routing advantages**:
  • Adapts to network changes automatically
  • Scales well
  • Reduces manual errors
Most networks use a combination: static for default gateways/key routes, dynamic for everything else.
See Advanced for details: Static Routing → Configuration & Use Cases

Networking/Switching/Basics/Fundamentals.title: Layer2
Networking/Switching/Basics/Fundamentals.bodyMd:
# Introduction --- ### What Is Layer 2 Switching? --- A Layer 2 switch forwards frames based on the **destination MAC address** inside the Ethernet frame header. It operates within a single broadcast domain unless VLANs are configured. --- ## Physical Ports on a Layer 2 Switch --- Layer 2 switches provide physical interfaces such as: - Ethernet (RJ45) ports - SFP fiber ports - Console ports These ports connect: - Hosts (PCs, servers, IoT devices) - Routers - Other switches
Terminology: See Glossary → Hosts & Ports
--- ## Example Topology --- ### Switch to Router - Switch **GigabitEthernet0/0** - Connected to Router **GigabitEthernet0/0** This provides Layer 3 connectivity beyond the local LAN. ### Hosts to Switch - PC1 → g0/1 - PC2 → g0/2 Switches learn each host’s MAC address and store it in the MAC address table. --- ## Port Naming and Speed Indicators --- Examples: - FastEthernet0/1 → 100 Mbps - GigabitEthernet0/1 → 1 Gbps - TenGigabitEthernet0/1 → 10 Gbps The name reflects: - Interface type - Maximum speed - Logical port position --- ## How Switching Works --- Layer 2 switches perform three primary actions: 1. **Learning** - Reads source MAC address - Adds it to the MAC address table 2. **Forwarding** - Sends frame only to the port matching the destination MAC 3. **Flooding** - If destination is unknown, frame is sent to all ports except the source --- ## Collision and Broadcast Domains --- - Each switch port = separate collision domain - Entire switch (without VLANs) = one broadcast domain --- ## Limitations of Layer 2 Switching --- - Cannot route between IP networks - Cannot break broadcast domains without VLANs - Broadcast storms can impact performance ---

Networking/Switching/Basics/Fundamentals.title: MACAddressTable
Networking/Switching/Basics/Fundamentals.bodyMd:
# How a Switch Builds and Uses Its MAC Address Table
### What Is a MAC Address Table?
A MAC Address Table (also called a CAM table) is a switch’s internal mapping of: MAC Address → Switch Port MAC addresses (Media Access Control addresses) are the Layer 2 identifiers found in Ethernet frames. The term “MAC table” describes what is stored. The term “CAM table” refers to the hardware technology used to store and search those MAC addresses. CAM stands for Content Addressable Memory. It allows the switch to quickly look up a destination MAC address and determine the correct outgoing port.
### What the Table Contains
Field Purpose
MAC Address Identifies the device
Port Where the device was learned
VLAN (if configured) Separates broadcast domains
Aging Timer Removes stale entries over time
### How a Switch Processes a Frame
When a frame arrives on a switch port, the switch follows a strict order of operations:
1. **Learn (Always Happens First)**
  • The switch reads the source MAC address.
  • It adds or updates the MAC → Port mapping.
  • This learning occurs on every received frame.
  • Even if the destination is unknown.
  • Even if the frame will be flooded.
2. **Forward Decision**
  • The switch reads the destination MAC address.
  • It searches the MAC address table.
3. **If the Destination Is Found (Table Hit)**
  • The frame is forwarded only out the matching port.
  • This is called filtering.
4. **If the Destination Is Not Found (Unknown Unicast)**
  • The switch floods the frame out all ports except the one it arrived on.
  • During flooding, the switch temporarily behaves like a hub.
This process allows the switch to intelligently forward traffic inside a local network.
### Example Scenario
Suppose Host A (MAC AA-AA) sends a frame to Host D (MAC DD-DD).
  • Frame arrives on Fa0/1
  • Source MAC AA-AA is learned on Fa0/1
  • Switch looks up DD-DD in its table
  • If DD-DD maps to Fa0/4, the frame is forwarded only out Fa0/4
  • If DD-DD is not yet in the table, the frame is flooded (unknown unicast).
The switch does not examine IP addresses. It makes decisions entirely based on Layer 2 information in the Ethernet header.
### Why Entries Age Out
Switches automatically remove MAC entries after a period of inactivity. On many enterprise switches, the default aging time is approximately 300 seconds (5 minutes). If no frames are received from a learned MAC address within that time, the entry is removed from the table. If a known MAC address sends traffic again, the switch refreshes the aging timer for that entry. This prevents incorrect forwarding if:
  • A device disconnects
  • A device moves to another port
  • A cable is reconnected elsewhere
  • A topology change occurs
The aging timer is configurable and may vary by vendor. Aging ensures the MAC address table stays accurate and adaptive.
### Learning Is Continuous
A switch’s MAC address table is not preconfigured. It builds dynamically based on observed traffic.
  • Every received frame contributes learning.
  • Active devices continuously refresh their entries.
  • Inactive devices eventually age out.
This makes the switch a self-adjusting Layer 2 forwarding system.
### Analogy: Reception Desk
A switch behaves similarly to a reception desk: 1. A visitor walks in → The receptionist notes who they are (learn source MAC). 2. The visitor asks for someone → The receptionist checks the directory (destination lookup). 3. If found → The visitor is directed to the correct office (forwarding). 4. If not found → An announcement is made to the whole building (flooding). 5. If the visitor keeps returning → The receptionist remembers them (aging refresh). This illustrates how switches learn, verify, and adapt over time.
### Summary
The MAC address table enables efficient Layer 2 switching:
  • Learns from source MAC addresses
  • Forwards based on destination MAC addresses
  • Floods only when necessary
  • Ages out stale entries
It is the core mechanism that makes Ethernet switching intelligent instead of broadcast-based.

Networking/Switching/Basics/HardwareAndP.title: EthernetSpeedHistory
Networking/Switching/Basics/HardwareAndP.bodyMd:
# Evolution of Ethernet Speeds --- Need a refresher on what Mbps, Gbps, or Tbps mean?
See: Bandwidth
## Original Ethernet --- - Introduced in the 1970s and formalized in 1983 under the IEEE 802.3 standard. - Provided 10 Mbps speed. ## Fast Ethernet --- - Introduced in 1995 under the IEEE 802.3u standard. - Provided 10× the speed of the original Ethernet. ## Gigabit Ethernet --- - Introduced in 1999 under the IEEE 802.3ab standard. - Marked the beginning of Gigabit-speed networking for mainstream use. ## 10 Gigabit Ethernet --- - Standardized in 2002 as IEEE 802.3ae. - Designed for high-performance enterprise and data center use. ## 25 Gigabit Ethernet --- - Industry initiative launched in 2014 by the 25G Ethernet Consortium. - Standardized as IEEE 802.3by on June 30, 2016. - Provided a cost-effective upgrade path from 10 Gbps. ## 40 Gigabit Ethernet --- - Standardized in 2010 under IEEE 802.3ba. - Combines four 10 Gbps lanes to reach 40 Gbps. - Common in aggregation and core layers of large networks. ## 100 Gigabit Ethernet --- - Standardized alongside 40 Gbps Ethernet in 2010 under IEEE 802.3ba. - Adopted in large-scale enterprise, backbone, and cloud environments. ## 50 Gigabit Ethernet --- - Standardized as IEEE 802.3cd on December 5, 2018. - A midpoint between 40 and 100 Gbps, used in high-performance setups. ## 200 Gigabit Ethernet --- - Standardized as part of IEEE 802.3bs on December 6, 2017. - Offers high capacity for advanced data center applications. ## 400 Gigabit Ethernet --- - Standardized alongside 200GbE under IEEE 802.3bs on December 6, 2017. - Built for telecom backbones, cloud scale, and hyperscale data centers. ## 800 Gigabit Ethernet --- - Approved by the IEEE P802.3df Task Force on February 16, 2024. - Targets 5G, AI infrastructure, and research networks. ## 1 Terabit Ethernet --- - Still under research and development. - Meant to support future workloads like AI, IoT, and global hyperscale platforms. ## 1.6 Terabit Ethernet --- - Under development by the IEEE P802.3dj Task Force. - Expected to be completed by Spring 2026. - Will use 200 Gbps lanes for ultra-high-performance networking. ---

Networking/Switching/Basics/HardwareAndP.title: SwitchesAndConnectors
Networking/Switching/Basics/HardwareAndP.bodyMd:
# Introduction --- Switches provide physical interfaces that allow devices to connect to a network. This page focuses on: - Switch hardware types - Port density and scalability - SFP ports and uplinks - The role of physical connectivity in Layer 2 switching For detailed cabling specifications, see: - Physical → Cabling & Interfaces → Ethernet Cabling Categories - Physical → Cabling & Interfaces → Copper Connectors - Physical → Cabling & Interfaces → Fiber Connectors & Transceivers --- ## Networking Switches Categorized by Size and Complexity --- ### 1. Small Switches --- - Typically compact with a smaller number of ports (e.g., 4 to 12 ports, often around 8 ports). - Designed for SOHO setups or environments with minimal network requirements. - Do not include advanced interconnection or management features. - Example: Desktop switches or unmanaged switches. ### 2. Medium-Sized Switches --- - Port count typically ranges from 12 to 48 ports. - Commonly found in access layers of networks for medium-sized businesses or departments. - Often rack-mountable. - Usually include managed capabilities. - May support stacking to expand logical port capacity. --- ### 3. Large Standalone Switches - Port counts often exceed 48 ports, going up to 96 ports or more in a single unit. - Typically used in larger networks such as distribution or core layers of enterprise environments. - Designed for higher throughput and greater scalability. --- ## Port Expansion Methods --- ### 4. Port Expansion Through Stacking --- - Stackable switches can be physically interconnected to act as a single logical unit. - Example: Stacking eight 48-port switches results in a **384-port logical switch**. - Provides scalability while maintaining centralized management. - Reduces operational complexity compared to managing multiple independent switches. ### 5. Port Expansion Through Modular / Chassis-Based Design --- - The most scalable and complex option. - Allows additional rows of ports by adding line cards or blades. - Each card provides multiple additional interfaces. - Total port counts can exceed **1000+ ports**. - Ideal for large-scale enterprise environments, data centers, or ISP networks. - Designed for flexibility, high performance, and long-term growth. --- ## SFP Ports and Uplinks --- Many enterprise switches include: - RJ45 Ethernet ports (copper) - SFP, SFP+, or SFP28 ports (fiber or copper modules) ### Common SFP Types --- - **SFP** → Up to 1 Gbps - **SFP+** → Up to 10 Gbps - **SFP28** → 25 Gbps SFP ports are commonly used for: - Switch-to-switch uplinks - Distribution-layer connections - Fiber backbone links
See: Physical → Cabling & Interfaces → Fiber Connectors & Transceivers
--- ## Copper vs Fiber Uplinks --- Switch uplinks may use: - Copper Ethernet (shorter distances) - Fiber optic links (longer distances, higher bandwidth) Fiber is commonly used in: - Backbone connections - Inter-building links - Data centers --- ## Virtual Switching --- Virtual switches exist inside hypervisors such as VMware vSphere. They allow: - VM-to-VM communication - VM-to-physical network connectivity Types: - Standard Switch (vSS) - Distributed Switch (vDS) Virtual switches perform Layer 2 forwarding but operate entirely in software. ---

Networking/Wireless/Basics/802_11Fundame.title: FrequenciesAndChannels
Networking/Wireless/Basics/802_11Fundame.bodyMd:
# Wireless Spectrum Basics
Wireless networking technologies use portions of the electromagnetic spectrum to transmit data without physical cables. Different technologies operate at different frequencies depending on: - Required range - Throughput needs - Regulatory rules Certain portions of the spectrum can be used without a license, provided devices follow power and protocol limitations.
## Licensed vs Unlicensed Spectrum
Spectrum Type Description
Licensed Reserved and regulated for specific providers (for example, cellular carriers).
Unlicensed Open for public use under defined power and usage rules (for example, Wi-Fi and Bluetooth).
Most home wireless technologies operate in unlicensed bands.
### Common Home Wireless Bands
Frequency Band Typical Uses
2.4 GHz Wi-Fi (802.11 b/g/n), Bluetooth, various IoT devices
5 GHz Wi-Fi (802.11 a/n/ac/ax)
The 2.4 GHz band: - Provides greater range - Has fewer non-overlapping channels - Is more prone to congestion The 5 GHz band: - Supports more non-overlapping channels - Offers higher throughput potential - Typically provides shorter range than 2.4 GHz
### Bluetooth vs Wi-Fi in 2.4 GHz
Both Bluetooth and Wi-Fi may operate in the 2.4 GHz band, but they are designed for different purposes.
Technology Primary Design Goal
Bluetooth Low power, short-range device connectivity
Wi-Fi (802.11) Higher throughput, LAN and internet access
Wi-Fi typically uses higher transmit power and supports infrastructure-based networking through access points.
## Channels Within Frequency Bands
Each frequency band is divided into smaller segments called channels. Channels allow multiple wireless networks to operate within the same band. Proper channel selection helps reduce: - Interference - Channel overlap - Performance degradation Channel planning becomes especially important in dense environments such as apartments, offices, and campuses.
### Why Band Selection Matters
Choosing between 2.4 GHz and 5 GHz impacts: - Speed - Coverage area - Interference levels - Device compatibility Modern wireless routers may support band steering, encouraging capable devices to connect to 5 GHz when appropriate.
See: Wireless → Advanced → RF & Performance → Band Steering & Load Balance

Networking/Wireless/Basics/802_11Fundame.title: WirelessStandards
Networking/Wireless/Basics/802_11Fundame.bodyMd:
# Wireless Communication Standards
A number of standards have been developed to ensure that wireless devices can communicate reliably and consistently. These standards define the: - radio frequency (RF) spectrum used - Data transmission rates - Modulation methods - Channel structure - Interoperability requirements Without standardized rules, devices from different manufacturers would not be able to communicate effectively.
### The Role of IEEE
The primary organization responsible for developing wireless technical standards is the Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers (IEEE). The IEEE 802.11 standard governs the Wireless LAN (WLAN) environment. The 802.11 standard includes multiple amendments that define: - Improvements in speed - Efficiency enhancements - Security updates - New frequency band usage Wireless LAN standards commonly operate in: - 2.4 GHz - 5 GHz Newer generations expand into additional frequency ranges, but 2.4 GHz and 5 GHz remain foundational bands in WLAN environments. Collectively, IEEE 802.11 wireless LAN technologies are commonly referred to as Wi-Fi.
### The Wi-Fi Alliance
While the IEEE creates technical standards, another organization known as the Wi-Fi Alliance focuses on certification and interoperability testing. The Wi-Fi Alliance: - Tests wireless devices from different manufacturers - Verifies compliance with IEEE standards - Ensures cross-vendor compatibility If a device displays the Wi-Fi logo, it means the equipment has been certified and should operate properly with other Wi-Fi certified devices.
### Why Standards Matter
Wireless standards continuously evolve to improve: - Speed - Efficiency - Capacity - Security - Spectrum utilization Manufacturers rapidly adopt new standards in their products, which makes it important to stay aware of: - Which standards your equipment supports - Compatibility between devices - Performance expectations Understanding wireless standards helps with: - Router selection - Network upgrades - Troubleshooting compatibility issues
### Network Mode (Single vs Mixed)
Wireless access points can be configured to support specific 802.11 standards. Examples include: - 802.11b - 802.11g - 802.11n - Mixed mode If all wireless devices use the same 802.11 standard, maximum performance for that standard can be achieved. If the access point is configured to allow only one standard, devices that do not support that standard cannot connect. A mixed mode environment allows older and newer wireless devices to connect to the same network. This improves compatibility but may reduce overall efficiency depending on device behavior.
### Reflection Questions
Consider the following: - Do you have a wireless network in your home? - What IEEE 802.11 standards does your wireless router support? - Are your client devices capable of using the same standards? Knowing this information helps evaluate performance limits and upgrade opportunities.

Networking/Wireless/Basics/WLANArchitect.title: BSSAndESSAndSSID
Networking/Wireless/Basics/WLANArchitect.bodyMd:
# BSS, ESS, and SSID
## Overview
Wireless networks are organized using structured design models that define how devices connect and identify themselves. At a basic level:
  • A BSS (Basic Service Set) is a single wireless network area served by one access point.
  • An ESS (Extended Service Set) is multiple access points using the same network name to provide broader coverage.
  • An SSID (Service Set Identifier) is the name that identifies the wireless network.
### BSS (Basic Service Set)
A BSS is the basic building block of a Wi-Fi network. In most common deployments, a BSS is:
  • One access point (AP)
  • Wireless devices (stations / STAs) associated to that AP
  • A defined coverage area (often called a cell)
A typical home router with Wi-Fi enabled usually provides a single BSS per radio band.
### ESS (Extended Service Set)
An ESS is created when multiple access points work together to provide a single, larger wireless network. In an ESS:
  • Multiple APs provide coverage across a larger area.
  • The APs share the same SSID (network name).
  • Devices can move between AP coverage zones without manually choosing a new network.
This is commonly used in:
  • Offices
  • Schools
  • Hotels
  • Homes with mesh Wi-Fi systems
### SSID (Service Set Identifier)
An SSID is the name assigned to a Wireless Local Area Network (WLAN). It identifies which wireless network a device should join. Key characteristics:
  • Case-sensitive
  • Alphanumeric
  • Up to 32 characters
  • Sent in wireless management frames
All devices that wish to join the same WLAN must use the exact same SSID.
### SSID Broadcast
Wireless routers broadcast their configured SSID by default. This broadcast:
  • Allows devices to automatically discover available wireless networks.
  • Displays the network name in the list of available Wi-Fi networks.
If SSID broadcast is disabled:
  • The network name does not appear in automatic scan results.
  • Users must manually enter the SSID on their device.
Disabling SSID broadcast may reduce casual visibility, but it is not sufficient security. All wireless networks should use the strongest available encryption to restrict unauthorized access.

VoIP/Foundations/Basics/Fundamentals/Int.title: Introduction
VoIP/Foundations/Basics/Fundamentals/Int.bodyMd:
# What Is Internet Telephony?
Making telephone calls over the internet has become increasingly common. An internet telephony client uses peer-to-peer technology similar to instant messaging.
  • Internet telephony makes use of Voice over IP (VoIP) technology.
  • VoIP converts analog voice signals into digital data.
  • That digital voice data is encapsulated into IP packets, which carry the call through the network.
## Setting Up an Internet Telephony Client
1. Choosing a Username
  • Once the client software is installed, the user selects a unique name so that calls can be received from other users.
2. Audio Setup
  • Speakers and a microphone are required, either built-in or separate.
  • A headset is frequently plugged into the computer to serve as a phone.
## Making a Call
  • Calls to other users of the same service are made by selecting their username from a list.
  • A call to a regular telephone, either a landline or a cell phone, requires a gateway to access the Public Switched Telephone Network (PSTN).
  • Depending on the service, calls to the PSTN may have associated charges.
  • The specific protocols and destination ports used by an internet telephony application can vary depending on the software.
## VoIP Beyond Standalone Phone Clients
  • Early internet telephony was often a standalone, single-purpose client, its whole job was placing calls.
  • Voice and video calling in modern general-purpose messaging apps, such as Discord, Microsoft Teams, and WhatsApp, is also built on VoIP technology.
  • In these apps, VoIP is one feature among many, rather than the entire product.

Tools/CiscoPacketTracer/Basics/Fundament.title: InstallAndInterface
Tools/CiscoPacketTracer/Basics/Fundament.bodyMd:
# Cisco Packet Tracer - Install & Interface
Cisco Packet Tracer is a network simulation and visualization tool used to practice configuration and troubleshooting without needing physical equipment. It is designed for: - Students with no networking background - Students with some networking experience - Learners enrolled in courses that require Packet Tracer labs Packet Tracer lets you: - Build a network from scratch - Use pre-built sample networks - Complete guided lab activities and assignments - Practice introductory IoT and Cybersecurity workflows (depending on the activity) Packet Tracer is not a replacement for real hardware practice, but it is extremely useful for learning concepts, testing ideas, and building confidence before touching physical gear.
### Download and Install
Packet Tracer is available for multiple platforms (commonly Windows, macOS, and Linux). General install flow: - Download the installer for your platform from Cisco Networking Academy resources - Run the installer - Launch Packet Tracer from your installed applications If prompted to authenticate on first launch, you may be asked to sign in using a Cisco / NetAcad / Skills For All workflow depending on your access method.
### Where to Get Help
If you get stuck during download, install, or first launch, these official resources are usually the fastest path to a working setup: - NetAcad Help / FAQs: https://www.netacad.com/help - NetAcad Lab Downloads / Packet Tracer downloads: https://www.netacad.com/resources/lab-downloads Some NetAcad pages include a virtual assistant (often shown as a chat widget) that can help with: - Packet Tracer questions - Certificates and badging questions - Common troubleshooting paths
### System Requirements
Exact requirements can vary by version, but typical considerations include: - A supported operating system version - Sufficient RAM for simulations - Adequate disk space for the application and saved labs If Packet Tracer feels sluggish, the most common causes are: - Very large topologies (many devices/links) - Low system memory - Running multiple heavy apps while simulating
### Interface Overview
Packet Tracer is built around a few core interface areas: - Menu bar: file actions, preferences, and tools - Workspace: where you build the topology - Device palette: routers, switches, end devices, cables, and more - Device configuration panel: physical/config/CLI tabs (depends on device) - Mode controls: Realtime vs Simulation (used heavily in troubleshooting labs) A simple first goal is to learn where to: - Place devices - Add cables - Open device tabs - Access the CLI - Save and reopen projects
