Tools/CiscoPacketTracer/Basics/Fundament.title: ProjectsAndSaves
Tools/CiscoPacketTracer/Basics/Fundament.bodyMd:
# Packet Tracer - Projects & Saves
Packet Tracer files are how you store topologies, configurations, and activity work so you can reopen them later or share them with others.
### Common File Types
File Type What It Is Used For
.pkt Standard project file (topology + device configs + saved state)
.pka Packet Tracer activity file (often includes instructions + grading logic)
.pkz Compressed project bundle used for sharing collections of Packet Tracer content
### Saving Best Practices
Recommended habits: - Save early, then save often - Use meaningful names so older versions are easy to identify - Keep one folder per lab or concept to avoid mixing unrelated files A simple naming pattern that stays readable: - topic-shortname + lab-number + version - Example idea (pattern only): routing-lab01-v1, routing-lab01-v2, routing-lab02-v1 If you are following a guided activity (.pka), consider saving your work as a separate .pkt copy as well (when allowed), so you can reuse the topology later without the activity wrapper.
### Version Compatibility Tips
Packet Tracer versions can differ in: - Device availability - Features and menus - Activity grading behavior If someone says “this file won’t open,” the most common causes are: - The project was created in a newer Packet Tracer version - The activity expects a specific version - Missing device support in the older installation Best practice when sharing: - Include your Packet Tracer version in a README note - Keep a copy exported/saved in the version your class or group is using
### Organizing Labs Like a Pro
A clean folder structure makes reviewing labs later much easier: - Group by topic (switching, routing, ACLs, VLANs, etc.) - Inside each topic, group by lab or lesson - Keep a short README note describing what the lab demonstrates This also makes it easier to sync to cloud storage or GitHub without losing context.
### Saving Packet Tracer Files to GitHub
Packet Tracer files (.pkt/.pka/.pkz) are typically binary files, which means: - Git can store them, but it cannot show meaningful line-by-line diffs - You should rely on versioning, commit messages, and documentation Recommended workflow: - Store Packet Tracer files inside a dedicated folder in your repo - Add a README next to them describing: - Lab goal - Key commands used - What the topology demonstrates - Any assumptions (IP plan, VLANs, routing protocol, etc.) If files become large or you have many versions, consider using Git LFS so your repository stays manageable. Practical collaboration tip: - Treat Packet Tracer files like “build artifacts” and document the changes in the README: - “Added VLAN 10/20 and trunk ports” - “Converted static routes to OSPF” - “Fixed default gateway on PCs” - “Validated with simulation PDU tests”
### 📎 Related Topics

Tools/Wireshark/Basics/Filters/Capture.title: Capture
Tools/Wireshark/Basics/Filters/Capture.bodyMd:
# Wireshark - Capture Filters
Capture filters reduce what Wireshark captures in the first place. They are useful when you want smaller captures, but they are less flexible than display filters because you cannot “bring back” packets that were never captured.
### Capture Filter Basics
Capture filters commonly use a BPF-style syntax (Berkeley Packet Filter). Typical use cases: - Capturing only DNS traffic - Capturing only traffic for one host - Capturing only a specific port
### Common Examples
- DNS only: udp port 53 - HTTP only: tcp port 80 - HTTPS only: tcp port 443 - One host (either direction): host 192.168.1.10 - Traffic to/from a network: net 192.168.1.0/24 - ICMP only: icmp
### When to Use Capture Filters
Good times to use capture filters: - You are on a very busy network and only need a single protocol - You are capturing for a long time and want smaller files - You are troubleshooting one device and want only its traffic If you are learning or doing general analysis, it is often better to: - Capture broadly - Use display filters to drill down later

Tools/Wireshark/Basics/Filters/Display.title: Display
Tools/Wireshark/Basics/Filters/Display.bodyMd:
# Wireshark - Display Filters
Display filters narrow what you see in the packet list without changing what was captured. They are the primary way to focus on specific traffic in a large capture.
### Quick Examples
Common display filters: - DNS traffic: dns - HTTP traffic: http - TLS traffic: tls - ICMP (ping): icmp Filter by IP address: - Source or destination equals IP: ip.addr == 192.168.1.10 - Source only: ip.src == 192.168.1.10 - Destination only: ip.dst == 8.8.8.8 Filter by port: - TCP port 443: tcp.port == 443 - UDP port 53: udp.port == 53
### Combining Filters
Use logical operators: - AND: and - OR: or - NOT: not Examples: - DNS for a specific host (where visible): dns and frame contains "example.com" - Web traffic from one device: ip.addr == 192.168.1.10 and (http or tls)
### Why Filters Matter
Filters help you: - Isolate a protocol (DNS/HTTP/TCP) - Follow one conversation (single client/server pair) - Reduce noise in busy environments - Spot failures (no response, retransmissions, resets, etc.)

Tools/Wireshark/Basics/Fundamentals/Capt.title: CaptureInterfaces
Tools/Wireshark/Basics/Fundamentals/Capt.bodyMd:
# Wireshark - Capture Interfaces
Capturing packets starts with selecting the correct network interface. Common interfaces you might see: - Ethernet (wired) - Wi-Fi (wireless) - VPN adapters - Virtual machine adapters - Loopback (host-only traffic, if supported/configured)
### Choosing the Right Interface
A quick way to pick the correct interface: - Look for the interface showing activity (packets moving) - Match the interface type to your connection (Wi-Fi vs Ethernet) - Disable or ignore unused adapters to reduce confusion If your capture shows “nothing,” common causes include: - Wrong interface selected - Traffic is occurring on a different adapter (VPN/VM) - You are only expecting traffic that is encrypted or not being generated - Permissions/capture driver issues (especially on Windows)
### Capture Workflow Basics
Typical workflow: - Select the correct interface - Start capture - Generate the traffic you care about (browse a site, run a ping, do a DNS lookup, etc.) - Stop capture - Use display filters to isolate relevant packets
### Saved Captures
Wireshark can open previously captured traffic (commonly .pcap or .pcapng files). This is useful when: - You want to share a capture with someone else - You want to repeat analysis without re-capturing - You are working a problem from logs/lab files

Tools/Wireshark/Basics/Fundamentals/Inst.title: InstallAndProfiles
Tools/Wireshark/Basics/Fundamentals/Inst.bodyMd:
# Wireshark - Install & Profiles
Wireshark is a network protocol analyzer used to capture and inspect network traffic. Common reasons to use Wireshark: - Troubleshooting network issues - Analyzing traffic flow and patterns - Debugging protocol behavior It can capture traffic in real time or open previously saved capture files for analysis.
### Install Basics
Wireshark is available on multiple platforms. General install notes: - Use the official Wireshark download for your OS - On Windows, packet capture typically requires Npcap (commonly installed alongside Wireshark) - On Linux/macOS, packet capture permissions may require additional setup depending on how your system is configured After installation, confirm: - Wireshark launches correctly - Your capture interfaces appear in the capture screen - You can start and stop a test capture
### What Profiles Are
Wireshark profiles let you save and switch between different interface configurations, such as: - Custom column layouts - Coloring rules - Display filter buttons - Protocol preferences and decode settings This is useful when you want different setups for different tasks, for example: - A profile focused on DNS troubleshooting - A profile focused on TCP performance - A profile for security review and suspicious traffic
### Suggested Starter Profiles
A simple starter set (optional): - Default: minimal changes - Troubleshooting: extra columns (Source/Dest, Protocol, Info, TCP stream) + useful coloring - Web: HTTP/HTTPS focus, SNI/Host visibility where possible

Networking/Foundations/Basics/DevicesAnd.title: HomeNetworkArchitecture
Networking/Foundations/Basics/DevicesAnd.bodyMd:
# Home Network Architecture --- ## Public Network vs Private Network --- Most home networks consist of two separate networks: - The public network (Internet Service Provider / ISP) - The private local network inside the home The public network delivers internet service into the home. The private network allows devices inside the home to communicate with each other and access the internet. A router separates these two networks. --- ## How Internet Service Physically Enters the Home --- Internet service can arrive using different physical technologies depending on the provider.
Delivery Type Physical Medium
Cable Internet RG-6 Coaxial Cable (DOCSIS)
DSL Telephone Line
Fiber Single-Mode Fiber (SC/APC connector)
Fixed Wireless / 5G External Antenna / Radio Link
Each delivery type requires a device that converts the ISP signal into standard Ethernet for the home network. --- ## Signal Conversion: Modem vs ONT ---
### Cable (DOCSIS)
Cable internet uses a cable modem. - Coaxial cable enters the home. - The modem converts radio-frequency electrical signals into Ethernet. - Ethernet connects to the router.
Physical Media Details: TCP/IP Model → Network Access → Physical → Cabling & Interfaces → Coaxial Cable
--- ### Fiber (GPON / FTTH) Fiber internet uses an Optical Network Terminal (ONT). - Fiber optic cable carries light signals into the home. - The ONT converts optical signals into Ethernet. - Ethernet connects to the router. Some providers combine the ONT and router into one integrated device. --- ## WAN vs LAN (Network Separation) --- After signal conversion, the router creates two logical sides: - WAN (Wide Area Network) → Public Internet - LAN (Local Area Network) → Private home network The router performs: - Network separation - NAT (Network Address Translation) - Packet forwarding - Basic firewall functions This separation protects internal devices from direct exposure to the public network. --- ## Integrated Home Routers (Gateway Devices) --- Many home networking devices combine multiple functions: - Modem or ONT - Router - Ethernet switch - Wireless Access Point These are commonly called: - Wireless routers - Gateway devices - Modem/router combos Internally, they still perform separate networking roles even though they appear as one device. --- ## Internal Network Distribution --- Home routers typically include: - One Internet / WAN port - Multiple LAN Ethernet ports - Built-in Wireless Access Point (Wi-Fi) Wired and wireless devices usually share the same LAN network. The router assigns private IP addresses using DHCP. --- ## Basic Traffic Flow Inside a Home --- 1. A device connects via Ethernet or Wi-Fi. 2. The router assigns a private IP address. 3. Internal LAN traffic stays inside the network. 4. Internet-bound traffic is sent to the router. 5. The router forwards traffic to the ISP via the modem or ONT. This architecture allows local communication and internet access while maintaining network separation. --- ## Modern Variation: Mesh Networking --- Some homes use mesh networking systems. In a mesh system: - One primary node connects to the ISP. - Additional nodes extend wireless coverage. - All nodes share the same LAN network. Mesh systems improve coverage without changing core network architecture. ---
← Back

Next →
Foundations - Devices & Models - Networking Models

Networking/Foundations/Basics/DevicesAnd.title: NetworkDevices
Networking/Foundations/Basics/DevicesAnd.bodyMd:
# Core Networking Devices --- ## Networking Devices Overview --- Networking devices are intermediate devices that connect end devices and control how traffic flows across a network. They do not usually generate user data themselves. Instead, they: - move traffic - direct traffic - filter traffic - manage connectivity These devices operate at different OSI layers depending on their function. --- ## Switch --- A switch connects devices within the same local network (LAN).
Switch Details
Primary Function Forwards frames between devices on a LAN
OSI Layer Layer 2 (Data Link)
Common Use Internal communication inside homes or offices
--- ## Router --- A router connects different networks together.
Router Details
Primary Function Routes packets between networks
OSI Layer Layer 3 (Network)
Common Use Connecting a LAN to the internet
--- ## Firewall --- A firewall controls and filters traffic for security.
Firewall Details
Primary Function Filters traffic based on rules
OSI Layers Layers 3–7 (depending on type)
Common Use Protecting internal networks from threats
--- ## Access Point (AP) --- An access point provides wireless connectivity to a network.
Access Point Details
Primary Function Extends network access via Wi-Fi
OSI Layer Layer 2 (Data Link)
Common Use Wireless connectivity in homes and offices
--- ## Modem --- A modem connects a local network to an internet service provider (ISP).
Modem Details
Primary Function Converts signals between ISP and local network
OSI Layers Layer 1–2
Common Use Home or business ISP connection
--- ## How These Devices Work Together --- In a small network: 1. End devices connect to a switch or access point. 2. The switch forwards traffic internally. 3. The router sends traffic to external networks. 4. The firewall filters traffic. 5. The modem connects the network to the ISP. Each device performs a specialized function in ensuring traffic moves efficiently and securely. ---

Next →
Foundations - Devices & Models - Home Network Architecture

Networking/Foundations/Basics/DevicesAnd.title: NetworkInfrastructure
Networking/Foundations/Basics/DevicesAnd.bodyMd:
# Introduction --- ### What Is Network Infrastructure? --- A network infrastructure is the foundation that allows devices to communicate. It includes the devices and the media used to carry data from one endpoint to another. Network infrastructure is commonly grouped into three categories: - End Devices - Intermediate Devices - Network Media --- ### End Devices --- End devices are where communication begins or ends. They act as the source or destination of network traffic. In order to uniquely identify hosts on a network, each device uses addressing (such as MAC and IP addresses) defined in higher networking layers.
End Device Common Purpose
Laptop / Desktop User access to applications and network services
Smartphone / Tablet Mobile communication and cloud access
IP Phone Voice communication over an IP network
Printer Shared printing over the network
Server Provides centralized services and resources
--- ### Intermediate Devices --- Intermediate devices connect end devices together and move traffic through the network. They provide: - switching and forwarding - routing between networks - segmentation and traffic control - security filtering and access control
For deeper detail, see: Foundations - Devices & Models - Networking Devices
--- ### Network Media --- Network media is the path that carries signals between devices. Examples include: - Copper cabling - Fiber-optic cabling - Wireless radio (Wi-Fi / cellular)
For deeper detail, see:
  • TCP/IP Model → Network Access → Physical → Signals & Media → Introduction
  • TCP/IP Model → Network Access → Physical → Signals & Media → Bit/Digital Signals
  • TCP/IP Model → Network Access → Physical → Transmission Concepts → Communication Standards
  • TCP/IP Model → Network Access → Physical → Transmission Concepts → Data Transmission Methods
--- ### Network Media by Scope --- Network media can also be categorized by how far communication travels and who controls the infrastructure.
Category Description
LAN (Local Area Network) Connects devices within a limited area such as a home, office, or campus.
WAN (Wide Area Network) Connects multiple LANs across cities, countries, or globally.
Wireless Media Uses radio transmission instead of physical cabling (see Wireless section).
Cloud Represents external networks or services outside the local infrastructure (see Cloud section).
--- ### How Data Moves Through Infrastructure --- Network communication follows a predictable path: 1. Data originates at an end device. 2. It travels across network media. 3. It passes through intermediate devices. 4. It arrives at the destination end device. Infrastructure provides the stable and reliable platform that allows this communication to occur — whether across a single cable or across global networks. --- ### Home Connection to an ISP (Typical Setup) --- Most home users do not see the infrastructure beyond their local network. For a home network, connecting to an ISP is usually straightforward, but the devices used matter for security.
Connection Option What It Means
Modem → Single Computer A direct connection to the ISP. This is not recommended because the device is exposed without a protective gateway.
Modem → Router (Most Common) The router provides a safer connection by acting as the network gateway and separating internal devices from the internet.
In many homes, the router is an integrated wireless router that includes: - a switch for wired devices - a wireless access point (AP) for Wi-Fi devices - basic services such as IP addressing for internal hosts --- ### Summary --- - Network infrastructure consists of end devices, intermediate devices, and network media. - End devices generate or receive traffic. - Intermediate devices move traffic and enforce connectivity/security. - Media carries the signals that transport data. - LAN, WAN, Wireless, and Cloud describe how infrastructure is organized and scaled. ---

# Networking Models --- ## What Is a Networking Model? --- A networking model provides a structured way to understand how networks are organized and how devices interact. Models help explain: - how communication is structured - how responsibilities are divided - how networks scale - how complexity is managed --- ## Network Scale and Design --- Different environments require different network designs.
Environment Characteristics
Home Network Small scale, few devices, often combined router/AP/modem
Enterprise Network Large scale, many devices, specialized hardware roles
Enterprise networks separate functions across dedicated devices to improve: - performance - reliability - security - scalability --- ## Traffic and Security Differences ---
Factor Typical Difference
Traffic Volume Enterprise networks handle heavy, continuous traffic
Security Needs Enterprises require advanced filtering and segmentation
--- ## Evolving Network Design --- Modern networking continues to evolve. Common trends include: - AI-driven optimization - Wi-Fi 6 and Wi-Fi 7 - 5G integration - cloud-managed infrastructure As networks grow more complex, structured models become increasingly important. --- ## Why Models Matter --- Networking models prepare you for deeper frameworks such as: - The OSI Model - The TCP/IP Model They provide a logical way to: - break networking into layers - troubleshoot systematically - separate hardware and software responsibilities --- ## Summary --- - Networking models organize how networks are structured. - Home and enterprise environments differ significantly. - Larger networks require dedicated infrastructure. - Layered models allow complex systems to be understood logically. ---

Networking/Foundations/Basics/DevicesAnd.title: BasicRouterSetup
Networking/Foundations/Basics/DevicesAnd.bodyMd:
# Introduction --- ### Basic Home Router Setup (Real-World) --- Many wireless routers designed for home use include an automatic setup utility to configure basic settings. These utilities commonly require a **PC or laptop connected by Ethernet** to the router during initial setup. If no device is available with a wired connection, you may need to configure Wi-Fi on a laptop or tablet first to access the router. --- ### Connect to the Router (LAN, Not Internet/WAN) --- To connect to the router using a wired connection: 1. Plug an Ethernet patch cable into the network port on the computer. 2. Plug the other end into a **LAN** port on the router. 3. Do **not** plug the cable into the port labeled **Internet** or **WAN**. The Internet/WAN port is used to connect the router to ISP equipment such as a cable modem or DSL modem. --- ### Modem Connection Types (Quick Identifiers) --- Some home routers connect to a separate modem, while others may have a built-in modem. Common identifiers: - **Cable modem** connections typically use a coaxial connector. - **DSL** connections typically use a telephone-style cable (often an **RJ-11** connector). If a router has a built-in modem, verify the connection type matches the ISP service. --- ### Link Lights and IP Address --- After connecting the computer to the router: - Verify the NIC link lights indicate a working connection. - The computer needs an IP address to communicate with the router. Most home routers run a local DHCP server by default, so the computer should receive an IP address automatically. If the computer does not receive an IP address: - Check the router documentation. - Manually configure the client with: - a unique IP address - subnet mask - default gateway - DNS information --- ### Plan Before You Configure --- Before entering the router configuration utility (or configuring through a web browser), think through how the network will be used. You do not want to configure the router in a way that: - limits what you can do on the network later, or - leaves the network unprotected Common decisions to think about early: - Network name (SSID) strategy - Device compatibility (single standard vs mixed/legacy mode) - Guest access needs - Security posture (encryption and access control) --- ### SSID Naming (Avoid Oversharing) --- If SSID broadcasting is enabled, the SSID name will be visible to wireless clients within range. Avoid including identifying details in the SSID such as: - router brand or model - location/unit identifiers - personal information Wireless router models often have known defaults and documented weaknesses, so revealing model information is not recommended.
See: Wireless → WLAN Architecture → BSS/ESS/SSID
--- ### Standards Compatibility (Single vs Mixed) --- If all devices support the same wireless standard, performance can be optimized for that standard. If older devices do not support newer standards, enabling **mixed/legacy mode** allows them to connect. Mixed/legacy mode improves compatibility but can reduce overall efficiency depending on device behavior.
See: Wireless → 802.11 Fundamentals → Wireless Standards
---

Networking/Foundations/Basics/DevicesAnd.title: Interfaces
Networking/Foundations/Basics/DevicesAnd.bodyMd:
# Router Interfaces
## Router Interfaces as Network Boundaries
Each interface on a router connects to a separate network. The IPv4 address assigned to an interface identifies the directly connected local network.
  • Hosts on that network use the interface IP as their default gateway.
  • This allows the router to forward traffic from the local network to other networks or the Internet.
## Default Gateway Assignment on Hosts
Hosts must be configured with an IP address in the same network as their connected router interface. The default gateway is the router interface IPv4 address.
  • Can be configured statically on the host.
  • Most commonly provided dynamically via DHCP.
In wireless routers (common in small/home networks):
  • The router acts as a DHCP server on the LAN (internal) side by default.
  • It assigns private IPv4 addresses (RFC 1918 ranges) to local hosts.
  • It provides the subnet mask and its own LAN interface IPv4 address as the default gateway.
  • The LAN interface IP is typically the first host address in the network (e.g., 192.168.1.1).
This setup ensures all local hosts (wired or wireless) can forward traffic to the router for Internet access.
## Internal vs External Networks
Wireless routers separate two networks:
Network Side Description
Internal (Inside) Local LAN side — private addresses, hosts connected via Ethernet or Wi-Fi, not directly routable on the Internet.
External (Outside) WAN/Internet side — router acts as DHCP client to ISP, receives routable public IPv4 address for Internet access.
The router serves as the boundary between the internal private network and the external public Internet.

Routers → Roles & Types (default gateway concepts and operation)
Advanced → NAT & PAT → Concepts & Types (translation at the boundary for Internet access)

Networking/Foundations/Basics/DevicesAnd.title: PortTypes
Networking/Foundations/Basics/DevicesAnd.bodyMd:
# Introduction --- ### Purpose of Router Ports --- Home and small business routers separate two different networks: - The public network (Internet / ISP) - The private local network (LAN) Router ports reflect this separation.
See: Foundations → Devices & Models → Home Network Architecture
--- ### LAN (Ethernet) Ports --- Most home routers include multiple ports labeled: - Ethernet - LAN - 1, 2, 3, 4 These ports connect to the router’s internal switch. All devices plugged into LAN ports: - Share the same local network - Receive private IP addresses - Can communicate directly with each other Wired devices typically connect using RJ45 Ethernet cables. --- ### Internet / WAN Port --- Routers also include one dedicated port labeled: - Internet - WAN - Sometimes a globe icon This port connects the router to: - A cable modem - A DSL modem - An Optical Network Terminal (ONT) - A fixed wireless device The WAN port is logically separate from the LAN ports. Traffic entering this port is considered external to the home network. --- ### Logical Network Separation --- In default configurations: - LAN ports and Wi-Fi belong to the same local network. - The WAN port belongs to a different network. The router performs: - Network Address Translation (NAT) - Packet forwarding - Basic firewall filtering This separation protects internal devices from direct exposure to the public internet. --- ### Integrated Wireless Access Point --- Most home routers include: - A built-in Ethernet switch - A built-in wireless access point Wireless devices connect through radio signals but join the same LAN as wired devices.
See: Wireless → WLAN Architecture → BSS/ESS/SSID
--- ### Typical Port Layout --- A typical home router may include:
Port Type Function
WAN / Internet Connects to ISP equipment (modem or ONT).
LAN Ethernet Connects internal wired devices.
Internally, the device performs multiple networking roles even though it appears as a single unit. --- ### Relationship to Switching --- The LAN ports function similarly to a small Layer 2 switch built into the router. For larger networks, a dedicated switch may connect to one of the LAN ports to expand capacity.
See: Switching → Hardware & Port Types → Switches and Connectors
---

Networking/Foundations/Basics/DevicesAnd.title: RolesAndTypes
Networking/Foundations/Basics/DevicesAnd.bodyMd:
# Router Roles and Types – Gateways to Other Networks
## Routers as Gateways
A router serves as a **gateway** — the exit point for traffic leaving one local network to reach remote networks or the Internet. It acts as the boundary between networks, connecting local hosts to destinations beyond their own subnet.
  • Analogy: The default gateway is like "the door out of the room" — to leave the local network (room), traffic must pass through this door (router).
  • Routers forward packets between different networks based on destination IP addresses.
  • Every host intending to communicate with remote destinations must know its default gateway address.
## Default Gateway Purpose and Operation
The **default gateway** is the IPv4 address of the router interface connected to the host's local network. Hosts send packets to this address when the destination is not on the local subnet.
  • Hosts determine if a destination is local using binary ANDing: source IP AND subnet mask compared to destination IP AND subnet mask.
  • If network portions match → packet sent directly (local delivery).
  • If network portions differ → packet sent to default gateway for forwarding.
  • Host then ARPs for the MAC address of the default gateway to encapsulate the frame.
The default gateway is usually the router interface closest to the host on the path to the Internet or remote destination. Different LAN segments (e.g., network management vs accounting departments) use different router interfaces as their default gateway.
## Common Configuration Issues
Misconfiguration prevents remote communication.
  • Default gateway IP not on the same subnet as the host → host cannot ARP for it.
  • Incorrect IP address or subnet mask → host misjudges local vs remote destinations.
  • No default gateway configured → host can only reach local network devices.
Accurate IP address, subnet mask, and default gateway configuration is essential for proper local/remote determination.
See related concepts:
Routers → Interfaces (how interface IP becomes default gateway)
Advanced → NAT & PAT → Concepts & Types (gateway role in small network Internet access)

# Wireshark - Follow Streams & RTT
Wireshark can reconstruct conversations to help you troubleshoot end-to-end communication.
### Follow Streams
“Follow Stream” views help isolate a single conversation. Common stream types: - TCP streams (web sessions, many app protocols) - UDP streams (depends on protocol and visibility) Use follow streams to: - Trace request/response behavior - Confirm payload content (when not encrypted) - Verify session direction and timing
### RTT Concepts
RTT (Round-Trip Time) is the time it takes for a packet to go from a sender to a receiver and get a response back. RTT is useful for: - Detecting latency issues - Comparing “fast” vs “slow” sessions - Identifying delays caused by network path or congestion Symptoms you might see: - Slow handshake completion - Long gaps between request and response - Retransmissions after timeouts
### Practical Use Cases
Common troubleshooting goals: - Diagnose network latency patterns - Identify bottlenecks or delays in communication - Validate whether slow performance is network-related or app-related

Tools/Wireshark/Basics/ViewsAndTools/IOG.bodyMd:
# Wireshark - IO Graphs & Stats
Wireshark includes built-in statistics views that help you understand traffic patterns beyond individual packets.
### IO Graphs
IO graphs help visualize traffic over time. Common things to graph: - Packets per second - Throughput trends - Spikes during failures or congestion - Response bursts (like DNS storms or retries) Use IO graphs when you need to answer: - “When did the problem start?” - “Is traffic steady or bursty?” - “Did retransmissions spike?”
### Useful Statistics Views
Examples of stats that can be useful: - Protocol hierarchy (what protocols dominate the capture) - Conversations (top talkers, endpoints, and pairs) - Endpoints (who is talking the most) - TCP-related summaries (when available in your view/version)
### Troubleshooting Patterns
Wireshark stats can help diagnose: - Latency spikes (traffic bursts, retries) - DNS issues (repeated queries, missing responses) - Congestion behavior (high packet rate or large throughput shifts)

Tools/Wireshark/Basics/ViewsAndTools/Pac.title: PacketAndBytesAndTree
Tools/Wireshark/Basics/ViewsAndTools/Pac.bodyMd:
# Wireshark - Packet / Bytes / Tree
Wireshark organizes captured traffic into panes that help you move from “big picture” to “deep detail.”
### Packet List (Top Pane)
The packet list shows: - Time - Source and destination - Protocol - Summary “Info” field This is where you quickly spot patterns like: - Repeated DNS queries - TCP retransmissions - Connection resets - Unexpected protocol chatter
### Packet Details (Tree View)
The packet details pane shows protocol layers and fields. This is where you inspect: - Ethernet headers - IP addressing - TCP/UDP ports - Application protocol fields (DNS/HTTP/etc.) Example DNS detail concepts you may see: - Query name - Response codes - Answer RRs (Resource Records) in responses
### Packet Bytes (Hex View)
The bytes pane shows the raw packet payload. This is useful when: - You need to confirm exact content - You want to see the raw encoded data - You are validating offsets and byte-level structure
### Navigating Conversations
Helpful navigation patterns: - Use “follow” style views to focus on a conversation - Use stream identifiers (TCP stream numbers) when available - Use packet relationships (request/response pairs) when visible in protocols like DNS

Networking/Legacy/OSIModel/Basics/Fundam.title: Introduction
Networking/Legacy/OSIModel/Basics/Fundam.bodyMd:
# Introduction --- ### Why Two Models?
In the early days of networking, vendors built proprietary systems that could not communicate with one another. The need for standardized communication frameworks led to two influential models:
- OSI Model: a seven-layer reference model describing general networking functions - TCP/IP Model: a four-layer implemented protocol suite that powers modern internet communications
The OSI model focuses on defining what functions must occur in networking. The TCP/IP model focuses on how the Internet protocol suite implements those functions.
### Development Timeline (High Level)
Year Milestone
1981 Internet Protocol (RFC 791) published
1982 U.S. DoD adopts TCP/IP
1983 ARPANET transitions to TCP/IP (“Flag Day”)
1984 ISO publishes the OSI Reference Model (ISO 7498)
1990s+ TCP/IP dominates deployments; OSI becomes the teaching model
### Why Learn OSI If TCP/IP Runs the Internet?
The TCP/IP model visualizes how protocols in the TCP/IP suite interact. However, it does not fully describe every general function required for all networking communications. For example:
- The TCP/IP Network Access layer does not specify which physical signaling methods must be used - It does not define electrical, mechanical, or encoding standards - It assumes underlying technologies handle media access and bit transmission
The OSI model separates these responsibilities clearly:
- Layer 1 (Physical): electrical, mechanical, signaling, and transmission standards - Layer 2 (Data Link): framing, MAC addressing, media access, and error detection
In other words, OSI describes the necessary functional categories, while TCP/IP describes the specific protocol implementations used on the Internet.
### How the Models Relate
The TCP/IP protocol suite can be described using the OSI reference model:
- TCP/IP Internet layer aligns with OSI Network layer (Layer 3) - TCP/IP Transport layer maps directly to OSI Transport layer (Layer 4) - TCP/IP Application layer combines OSI Application, Presentation, and Session - TCP/IP Network Access layer combines OSI Data Link and Physical
OSI provides a structured conceptual lens. TCP/IP provides the operational protocol stack.
### Why OSI Is Still Taught
- Offers a clear troubleshooting framework (“Is this a Layer 1 issue or Layer 7 issue?”) - Separates networking into logical functional boundaries - Remains foundational in certifications and interviews
OSI is not about memorizing trivia — it is about learning to think in layers.

Networking/Legacy/OSIModel/Basics/Fundam.title: LayerComparison
Networking/Legacy/OSIModel/Basics/Fundam.bodyMd:
# Introduction --- ### OSI Layer Purposes
Layer Primary Purpose
7 – Application Provides network services to end-user applications.
6 – Presentation Data formatting, encoding, translation, compression, and encryption (conceptual responsibilities).
5 – Session Establishes, manages, and terminates dialogs (sessions) between communicating hosts.
4 – Transport End-to-end delivery, segmentation/reassembly, reliability, and flow control.
3 – Network Logical addressing and routing across interconnected networks.
2 – Data Link Framing, MAC addressing, local delivery, and basic error detection.
1 – Physical Transmission of raw bits over physical media (signals, voltage, timing, connectors).
### Concept vs Implementation
The OSI model describes what functions must exist in any complete networking system. The TCP/IP model describes which specific protocols implement those functions on modern networks. Key relationships:
- OSI Layers 1–2 are combined into TCP/IP Network Access - OSI Layer 3 maps to TCP/IP Internet - OSI Layer 4 maps directly to TCP/IP Transport - OSI Layers 5–7 are combined into TCP/IP Application
The OSI model provides a layered conceptual blueprint. The TCP/IP model provides the operational protocol stack.
### Why This Distinction Matters
The TCP/IP model visualizes how protocols in the Internet suite interact, but it does not describe every general networking responsibility in detail. For example:
- The TCP/IP Network Access layer does not formally define electrical signaling standards - It does not specify media encoding methods - It assumes lower-level technologies handle transmission details
The OSI model separates these responsibilities clearly:
- Layer 1 (Physical) explains how bits are transmitted over wire, fiber, or air - Layer 2 (Data Link) explains framing and local delivery
This is why OSI remains powerful as a learning and troubleshooting framework.
### Where to Study the Operational Details
Use the OSI model for conceptual structure. Study the TCP/IP pages for real protocol behavior, headers, ports, timers, and troubleshooting techniques.

Networking/Legacy/ProtocolsAndStatuses/B.bodyMd:
# What Is POP3?
POP3 stands for Post Office Protocol version 3. A server that supports POP3 clients receives and stores messages addressed to its users.
  • When the client connects to the email server, messages are downloaded to the client.
  • By default, messages are not kept on the server after they have been accessed by the client.
  • Clients contact POP3 servers on TCP port 110.
POP3 is now widely considered a legacy protocol, most major providers still support it, but recommend IMAP instead, since IMAP keeps mail synchronized across multiple devices rather than downloading it to just one.
See:
Application Layer → Email & Communication → IMAP

Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/Application.title: IMAP
Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/Application.bodyMd:
# What Is IMAP?
IMAP stands for Internet Message Access Protocol. A server that supports IMAP clients receives and stores messages addressed to its users, similar to POP3.
  • Unlike POP3, IMAP keeps messages in the mailbox on the server, unless the user deletes them.
  • This allows the same mailbox to be accessed consistently from multiple devices, since messages are not downloaded and removed.
  • The most current version, IMAP4, listens for client requests on TCP port 143.
Many different email servers exist for the various network operating system platforms, and most support IMAP alongside SMTP and, in many cases, POP3.

Legacy → POP3 → Introduction

Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/Application.title: SMTP
Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/Application.bodyMd:
# What Is Email?
Email is one of the most popular client/server applications on the internet. Email servers run server software that enables them to interact with clients and with other email servers over the network.
  • Each mail server receives and stores mail for users who have mailboxes configured on that server.
  • Each user with a mailbox uses an email client to access the mail server and read messages.
  • Many internet messaging systems use a web-based client, such as Microsoft 365, Yahoo, or Gmail.
  • Mailboxes are identified by the format user@company.domain.
Several application protocols are used in processing email, including SMTP, POP3, and IMAP4.
## What Is SMTP?
SMTP stands for Simple Mail Transfer Protocol. It is used by an email client to send messages to its local email server.
  • SMTP requests are sent to TCP port 25.
  • The local server decides whether a message is destined for a local mailbox or addressed to a mailbox on another server.
## How SMTP Delivers a Message
1. Client Sends to Local Server
  • The sender's email client uses SMTP to send the message to its local mail server.
2. Server Checks the Destination
  • If the recipient's mailbox is on that same server, the message is stored there directly.
3. Forwarding Between Servers
  • If the message is addressed to a mailbox on a different server, SMTP is used again, this time between the two mail servers, to forward the message.
4. Recipient Retrieves the Message
  • Once the message reaches the recipient's mail server, the recipient's email client retrieves it using POP3 or IMAP.

# What Is Text Messaging?
Text messaging is one of the most popular communication tools in use today. Text messaging software is built into many online applications, smartphone apps, and social media sites.
  • Text messages may also be called instant messages, direct messages, private messages, or chat messages.
  • Text messaging enables users to communicate in real-time over the internet.
  • On a computer, text messaging is usually accessed through a web-based client integrated into a social media or information sharing site, and these clients usually only connect to other users of the same site.
## Standalone Text Messaging Clients
A number of standalone text message clients also exist, available across a wide variety of operating systems and devices, typically including a mobile version.
  • Examples include Cisco Webex Teams, Microsoft Teams, WhatsApp, Facebook Messenger, Slack, and Discord, among many others.
  • In addition to text messages, these clients often support the transfer of documents, video, music, and audio files.

Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/Application.title: Fundamentals
Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/Application.bodyMd:
# Fundamentals
### Role-Based Communication
In modern networks, a host can operate as a client, a server, or both. The software installed on the device determines which role it performs at any moment. Client and server software often run on separate computers, but a single machine can run both simultaneously. Client and server software are built to follow agreed-upon standards and protocols. This is what allows a browser from one company to communicate successfully with a web server built by a completely different company, since both sides are following the same rules.
### Client vs Server (Core Definitions)
Role What It Does
Client Runs software that can request and use information or services from another host.
Server Runs software that can provide information or services to requesting hosts.
### One Computer, Many Services
A single computer can run multiple server programs. For example, in a home or small office, one device might operate as:
  • a file server
  • a web server
  • an email server
At the same time, that same machine may run several client applications. A user could:
  • check email
  • browse the web
  • send messages
  • stream audio
all at once, connecting to multiple servers simultaneously.
### Services Require Separate Server Software
Each network service is typically delivered by its own server application. Providing web pages is different from handling email or storing files, even when they run on the same physical system.
### Examples of Client Software
A familiar example of client software is a web browser. Common examples include:
  • Google Chrome
  • Microsoft Edge
  • Safari
  • Firefox
  • Brave
  • Vivaldi
The client sends a request and the server returns a response. Every site, download, or cloud service you reach comes from a server somewhere on the internet. Another example is email. A mail client communicates with a server so users can send and receive messages. File access is similar. A file server stores data in a central location, and clients retrieve it when needed.
### Client-Server Interaction
In client-server communication:
  • The client initiates a request to a server, for example a web browser requesting a webpage.
  • The server responds with the requested data or service.
  • Communication often involves multiple protocols, such as DNS for name resolution, TCP for reliable transport, and HTTP/HTTPS for web content.
Example workflow (web page request):
  • User enters a URL, for example www.example.com.
  • Client performs a DNS lookup to resolve the domain to an IP address.
  • Client establishes a TCP connection to the server IP on port 80 (HTTP) or 443 (HTTPS).
  • Client sends an HTTP request, for example GET /index.html.
  • Server responds with the webpage data.
The combination of IP address and port forms a socket, for example 192.168.1.5:5507 for the client and 172.16.10.50:80 for the server.
### URI, URL, and URN
These three terms describe ways to identify resources on a network, and they are related rather than interchangeable.
  • URI (Uniform Resource Identifier) is the general term for a string that identifies a resource. A URI is made up of a protocol/scheme, a hostname, a path/filename, and an optional fragment.
  • URL (Uniform Resource Locator) is a type of URI that specifies the actual network location of a resource. URLs are most commonly associated with HTTP and HTTPS, but the same format is also used with other protocols such as FTP, SFTP, and SSH.
  • URN (Uniform Resource Name) is a type of URI that identifies a resource by name only, within a namespace, without pointing to a specific location or referencing a protocol, for example urn:isbn:0451450523.
Every URL is a URI, but not every URI is a URL.
### Summary
  • Roles depend on the software.
  • A device may be a client, server, or both.
  • Shared standards and protocols are what let different vendors' client and server software work together.
  • Servers can support many clients.
  • One computer can run multiple services.
  • Clients often connect to multiple servers at the same time.
  • A URI is the general identifier, a URL is a URI that locates a resource, and a URN is a URI that only names a resource.

Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/Application.title: P2PAndExamples
Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/Application.bodyMd:
# Introduction --- ### Peer-to-Peer vs Client/Server --- In a client/server design, one host sends a request and another host returns a response that delivers information or a service. In a peer-to-peer (P2P) design, devices exchange resources more directly with one another instead of relying entirely on a single central system. A device can also perform both roles depending on what it is doing.
Model How Communication Happens
Client/Server Clients send requests; servers send responses.
P2P Devices share resources directly with each other.
--- ### How Basic P2P Networks Are Built --- The simplest form of P2P networking can involve just two computers connected by a wired or wireless link. When more devices are added, a network device such as a switch is typically needed so all peers can communicate. --- ### Performance Considerations --- When a machine must support its user while also responding to other devices, performance may decrease. Because of this, environments with heavy demand usually transition toward dedicated servers whose only job is to provide services. --- ### P2P Applications --- Some software is built so that each participant acts as both a client and a server within the same conversation. This means each device must: - provide a user interface - run a background service that can respond to other peers --- ### Hybrid Designs --- Many modern systems are not purely client/server or purely P2P. Instead, they use a hybrid model. In hybrid designs, centralized infrastructure may handle: - authentication - user accounts - directories or indexes - session coordination After peers discover each other, some communication or data transfer may happen directly between devices. --- ### Practical Hybrid Examples ---
Platform or Situation Why It Is Hybrid
Messaging & collaboration apps (Discord, Slack, Teams) Central servers manage login, identity, and routing, while media or data may travel between users.
Video meetings (Zoom, Teams) Central services coordinate participants; streams may be optimized between endpoints.
Mobile devices sharing data Cloud systems assist discovery and permissions, but devices can exchange information more directly.
--- ### Key Takeaways --- - Client/server defines clear requester and provider roles. - P2P encourages more direct sharing. - Devices frequently operate as both. - Real-world systems are often hybrid. - Growing demand usually leads toward dedicated infrastructure. ---

Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/Internet/Ad.title: ConceptsAndTypes
Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/Internet/Ad.bodyMd:
# Network Address Translation (NAT) Concepts and Types
## Purpose of NAT in Small Networks
Network Address Translation (NAT) allows devices on private IPv4 networks to access the public Internet by translating private (non-routable) source addresses to a public (routable) address. It solves IPv4 address exhaustion by letting many internal hosts share one or few public addresses.
  • Private networks use RFC 1918 reserved ranges that cannot route directly on the Internet.
  • NAT is performed on the boundary device (usually a wireless router or edge router).
  • Common in home/SOHO/small networks where ISPs provide only one public address.
## Private vs Public IPv4 Addresses
NAT is necessary because most internal networks use private IPv4 addresses (RFC 1918), which are not routable on the public Internet.
  • 10.0.0.0 – 10.255.255.255 (/8) — large enterprises
  • 172.16.0.0 – 172.31.255.255 (/12) — medium-sized networks
  • 192.168.0.0 – 192.168.255.255 (/16) — small/home/SOHO networks (most common for NAT/PAT)
Public IPv4 addresses are globally unique and routable across the Internet.
See detailed explanation of public/private addresses, special-use ranges, and allocation:
Internet Layer → Basics → IP Addressing → IPv4 → Types of Addresses
## NAT Operation Basics
NAT translates the source IP address of outgoing packets from private (inside local) to public (inside global), and reverses the destination IP on returning packets.
  • Outbound: Private source → public source (router maintains translation table).
  • Inbound: Public destination → private destination (using the table to forward correctly).
  • Only packets leaving the local network are translated; local traffic stays unchanged.
  • Wireless routers often receive public address on WAN via DHCP from ISP and assign private addresses on LAN via DHCP server.
Example flow (from whiteboard/video):
1. Host (192.168.1.15) sends to Internet server (210.100.5.5). 2. Router translates source to public (e.g., 200.100.58.51). 3. Return packet to 200.100.58.51 → router translates back to 192.168.1.15.
## Key NAT Terminology
  • Inside Local: Original private IPv4 address of the host on the internal network (e.g., 192.168.1.15).
  • Inside Global: Public IPv4 address representing the inside host to the external network (e.g., 200.100.58.51).
  • Outside Local: Address of outside host as seen from inside (usually same as outside global in basic NAT).
  • Outside Global: Public address of external host (e.g., 210.100.5.5).
## Types of NAT
NAT types vary by how mappings are created and how many hosts share addresses.
  • Static NAT: One-to-one permanent mapping (private to specific public). Used for servers needing consistent external access.
  • Dynamic NAT: Temporary one-to-one from a pool of public addresses. Requires enough public IPs for simultaneous sessions; rare in small networks.
  • PAT (NAT Overload): Many-to-one mapping using unique port numbers to differentiate sessions. Most common in small/home networks; allows hundreds/thousands of hosts to share one public address.
In small networks, PAT overload is the default on wireless routers.
See related concepts:
Foundations → Devices & Models → Routers → Interfaces (default gateway and boundary role)

Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/Application.title: DNS
Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/Application.bodyMd:
# DNS Basics
## What is DNS?
DNS stands for Domain Name System. DNS is a server-based system that associates a domain name, or a host name, with an IP address. It functions like a phonebook for the internet, translating human-readable domain names into machine-readable IP addresses.
  • The network itself only understands IP addresses, not domain names.
  • When a system needs to reach a domain, it does not know the IP address ahead of time, so it asks a DNS server to resolve it.
  • This resolution step is what allows people to use memorable names like www.example.com instead of having to know and type raw IP addresses.
  • Example:
    • Domain Name: www.example.com
    • IP Address: 192.0.2.1
  • On a home network, the DNS server address a device uses is not set manually, it is typically assigned automatically.
  • The ISP provides a DNS server address to the home router.
  • The router then passes that DNS server address to every device on the network using DHCP.
  • Whenever a device needs to resolve a name, such as www.example.com, its DNS client queries this configured server first, before sending the actual HTTP request.
## How DNS Works
1. Query Process
  • The client (e.g., browser) sends a query to a DNS resolver.
  • The resolver contacts DNS servers in a hierarchy:
    • Root Servers: Handle top-level domains (TLDs) like .com, .org.
    • TLD Servers: Manage specific domain extensions.
    • Authoritative Servers: Provide the IP address for a specific domain.
2. Response
  • The resolver returns the IP address to the client.
3. Using the Resolved IP Address
  • The host uses the returned IP address to contact the actual web server directly.
  • The web server responds with the content needed to display the requested page.
  • This next step is handled by application protocols such as HTTP/HTTPS, DNS's job ends once the IP address has been resolved.
See:
Application Layer → Web & Name Resolution → HTTP/HTTPS
## Key Components
1. DNS Records
  • A Record: Maps domain names to IPv4 addresses.
  • AAAA Record: Maps domain names to IPv6 addresses.
  • CNAME Record: Points one domain name to another domain name, called a canonical name, rather than directly to an IP address. Lookups often resolve through a chain of several CNAME records before reaching the final address.
  • MX Record: Specifies mail servers for a domain.
2. Resolvers
  • DNS resolvers are responsible for querying DNS servers on behalf of clients.
3. Caching
  • DNS resolvers cache query results to improve speed and reduce server load.
  • A result served from cache, rather than fetched fresh from an authoritative server, is labeled a non-authoritative answer in tools like nslookup.
### Example Workflow
1. Client
  • "What is the IP of www.example.com?"
2. DNS Resolver
  • "The IP is 192.0.2.1"
## Useful DNS Commands
### nslookup
nslookup queries DNS servers for specific information and can be used two ways: entering an interactive mode, or issuing a single direct query.
#### Entering Interactive Mode
Running nslookup alone, with no domain name, enters interactive mode.
```shell C:\>nslookup Default Server: Unknown Address: 10.10.10.1 ```
  • This output shows the DNS server currently configured on the client.
  • This address can be set manually, or learned automatically through DHCP.
#### Querying a Domain in Interactive Mode
Once in interactive mode, entering a domain name at the prompt queries the configured server directly.
```shell >www.cisco.com Server: UnKnown Address: 10.10.10.1 Non-authoritative answer: Name: e2867.dsca.akamaiedge.net Addresses: 2600:1404:a:395::b33 2600:1404:a:38e::b33 172.230.155.162 Aliases: www.cisco.com www.cisco.com.akadns.net wwwds.cisco.com.edgekey.net wwwds.cisco.com.edgekey.net.globalredir.akadns.net ```
  • The Aliases list shows a chain of CNAME records, each pointing to the next, before ultimately resolving to a real server.
  • Both IPv6 and IPv4 addresses can be returned in the same lookup.
  • The result is labeled non-authoritative, meaning it came from the resolver's cache rather than a fresh query to an authoritative server.
#### Exiting Interactive Mode
The exit command leaves interactive mode and returns to the normal command line.
```shell >exit ```
#### Direct Query Mode
A domain name can be added directly to the nslookup command for a single, one-shot query, without entering or exiting interactive mode.
```shell C:\>nslookup www.google.com Server: UnKnown Address: 10.10.10.1 Non-authoritative answer: Name: www.google.com Addresses: 2607:f8b0:4000:80f::2004 172.217.12.36 ```
#### nslookup on Linux
nslookup behaves the same way on Linux, though the default server address shown will depend on how that particular system resolves DNS locally.
```shell user@cisconetacad$nslookup Server: 127.0.1.1 Address: 127.0.1.1#53 >www.cisco.com Non-authoritative answer: www.cisco.com canonical name = www.cisco.com.akadns.net. www.cisco.com.akadns.net canonical name = wwwds.cisco.com.edgekey.net. wwwds.cisco.com.edgekey.net canonical name = wwwds.cisco.com.edgekey.net.globalredir.akadns.net. wwwds.cisco.com.edgekey.net.globalredir.akadns.net canonical name = e144.dscb.akamaiedge.net. Name: e144.dscb.akamaiedge.net Address: 23.60.112.170 >exit ```
  • Linux's nslookup spells out each step of the CNAME chain explicitly with canonical name =, rather than listing them together under a single Aliases heading, but it represents the same underlying resolution process.
```shell user@cisconetacad$nslookup www.google.com Server: 127.0.0.53 Address: 127.0.0.53#53 Non-authoritative answer: Name: www.google.com Address: 172.217.6.164 Name: www.google.com Address: 2607:f8b0:4000:812::2004 ```
### dig
dig retrieves detailed DNS query results.
  • Example: dig www.example.com
### ipconfig /displaydns
  • Shows cached DNS records on Windows.

Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/Application.title: HTTPHTTPS
Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/Application.bodyMd:
# What Are HTTP and HTTPS?
HTTP (Hypertext Transfer Protocol) and HTTPS (Hypertext Transfer Protocol Secure) are the foundation of web communication, enabling browsers to communicate with servers. Because HTTP and HTML are shared, open standards rather than something owned by any single company, web servers and browsers built by completely different manufacturers are still able to work together seamlessly.
### HTTP
  • Protocol for transferring hypertext data between a client (browser) and a server.
  • Operates on port 80.
  • Unencrypted, making it vulnerable to security breaches.
### HTTPS
  • A secure version of HTTP that encrypts communication using TLS/SSL protocols.
  • Operates on port 443.
  • Ensures data confidentiality, integrity, and authentication.
  • Visible to the user directly in the address bar, a secure page's address begins with https:// rather than http://.
## Key Features
1. Request-Response Model
  • Follows client-server architecture.
  • Example:
    • Client: Requests a webpage (GET request).
    • Server: Sends the requested webpage.
2. Stateless Protocol
  • HTTP treats each request as independent.
  • HTTPS ensures these stateless requests are secure.
3. Common HTTP Methods
  • GET: retrieve resources
  • POST: submit data to the server
  • PUT: update resources
  • DELETE: remove resources
### Example Workflow
1. Client Request ```shell GET /index.html HTTP/1.1 Host: www.example.com ``` 2. Server Response ```shell HTTP/1.1 200 OK Content-Type: text/html Content-Length: 1024 ```
## From IP Address to Rendered Page
Once DNS has resolved a domain name to an IP address, HTTP is what carries the actual request and response between the client and that server.
1. Requesting the Page
  • The client uses the resolved IP address to contact the web server directly.
  • The request itself is sent using HTTP.
2. Receiving HTML
  • The web server responds over HTTP with the page's HTML code, short for Hypertext Markup Language, the most commonly used markup language for encoding a page's content.
  • The browser downloads this HTML and renders it as the visible webpage.
3. Viewing the Raw HTML
  • Most browsers let you inspect the raw HTML a page was built from, commonly through a View Page Source option in the developer tools menu.
  • This shows the exact HTML that was downloaded from the server, before the browser applies any rendering.
See:
DevScriptStax → HTML → Basics → Fundamentals → Introduction
## Advantages of HTTPS
  • Encryption: prevents unauthorized access to data in transit
  • Authentication: verifies the server identity via certificates
  • Integrity: detects tampering of data between client and server
## Additional Tools for HTTP/HTTPS
  • curl: CLI tool to make HTTP/HTTPS requests
  • Postman: GUI tool for testing API requests

Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/Application.title: Introduction
Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/Application.bodyMd:
### What is the Application Layer --- The Application Layer in the TCP/IP model corresponds to **Layers 5, 6, and 7** of the OSI model. While the OSI model separates these into **Session**, **Presentation**, and **Application** layers, the TCP/IP model consolidates them into one unified layer. This layer serves as the **interface between network services and applications**. It ensures users and applications can interact with the network, sending and receiving data seamlessly. --- ### Key Responsibilities 1. **Interfacing with Applications**: - Provides an interface for applications to utilize network resources. - Examples: Web browsers accessing websites, email clients connecting to mail servers. 2. **Protocol Support**: Supports protocols that enable communication and data exchange. Examples of Protocols: - HTTP/HTTPS - DNS - FTP - SMTP - IMAP/POP3 3. **User Authentication**: - Handles authentication processes like username/password exchanges during logins. 4. **Session Management**: - Maintains and manages communication sessions between devices. --- ### Supported Protocols - HTTP/HTTPS: transfers web content between clients and servers (HTTPS adds encryption) - DNS: resolves human-readable domain names into IP addresses - FTP: file transfer between hosts - SMTP: sending email - IMAP/POP3: retrieving email from servers ---

Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/Application.title: FtpSftp
Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/Application.bodyMd:
# What Is FTP?
FTP stands for File Transfer Protocol. It provides an easy method to copy files from one device to another, from a client to a server, or from a server back to a client.
  • A host running FTP client software can access an FTP server to perform file management functions, including uploads and downloads.
  • FTP also allows a client to manage files remotely, sending commands such as delete or rename.
  • A common use case is uploading a website's files to a web server.
## How FTP Works
FTP uses two separate connections between client and server, rather than a single one.
1. Control Connection
  • Opened first, using destination TCP port 21.
  • Carries commands between client and server, such as login, navigation, or file management requests.
2. Data Connection
  • Opened once the session is established, using TCP port 20.
  • Carries the actual file data being transferred.
  • Based on commands sent across the control connection, data can be downloaded from the server or uploaded from the client.
## Accessing FTP
### Built-in Access
  • FTP client software is built into most computer operating systems.
  • Most web browsers also include basic FTP client functionality.
### Standalone FTP Clients
  • Standalone FTP clients offer more options through an easy-to-use, GUI-based interface.
  • One widely used example is FileZilla, a free, open-source, cross-platform client supporting FTP, SFTP, and FTPS.
  • FileZilla's installer has a history of bundling unwanted third-party software, only download it from the official project site, and decline any bundled offers during installation.
  • As an example, ftp.cdc.gov is a public FTP server that can be accessed anonymously using a client like FileZilla.
## Using a GUI FTP Client
Standalone FTP clients typically use a dual-pane layout, local files on one side, the remote server's files on the other, with drag-and-drop handling the actual transfer.
1. Connecting to a Server
  • Enter the server's host address, a username, and a password if required.
  • Typically, a real username and password are required so files can be uploaded and downloaded securely.
2. Transferring Files
  • Once connected, dragging a file from one pane to the other transfers it in that direction.
  • Dragging from the remote pane to the local pane downloads a file, dragging the other direction uploads one.
## Anonymous FTP
Some FTP servers are configured to allow public access without requiring real credentials, this is known as anonymous FTP.
  • The username is entered literally as anonymous, with no password required.
  • This differs from the typical case, where a real username and password are needed to access a server securely.
  • Some government and public data servers are configured this way, allowing anyone to connect without an account.

# Network Services Introduction
Network services are Application-layer protocols that provide essential functionality for end devices and network operations. They typically run over Transport layer protocols (TCP or UDP) and enable features such as configuration, synchronization, monitoring, and file transfer. These services support efficient network use and infrastructure management.
### Common Network Services
  • DHCP (Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol) — enables automatic assignment of IP addresses and network parameters to hosts. Covered in depth as part of IP Addressing fundamentals, rather than as a separate Network Services page.
  • NTP (Network Time Protocol) — synchronizes clocks across devices for accurate timekeeping.
  • SNMP (Simple Network Management Protocol) — allows monitoring and management of network devices and performance.
  • FTP/SFTP (File Transfer Protocol / Secure File Transfer Protocol) — provides mechanisms for transferring files between systems.
Each service operates over specific ports and follows client/server or request/response models.
### Detailed Content Locations
For in-depth explanations, message formats, and operation:
See:

Network Services → NTP
Network Services → SNMP
Network Services → FTP/SFTP
These services illustrate how the Application layer supports broader network functionality and user needs.

Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/Internet/Ba.title: DHCP
Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/Internet/Ba.bodyMd:
# DHCP (Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol)
DHCP is a protocol that automatically assigns IP configuration information to devices on a network. Instead of manually configuring an IP address, subnet mask, gateway, and DNS server, a device can obtain this information automatically from a DHCP server. ## Static vs Dynamic Addressing
IPv4 addresses can be assigned either statically or dynamically.
### Static Assignment
The network administrator must manually configure the network information for a host. At a minimum, this includes:
  • IP address: Identifies the host on the network.
  • Subnet mask: Used to identify the network on which the host is connected.
  • Default gateway: Identifies the networking device that the host uses to access the internet or another remote network.
#### Static Addresses
Static addresses have some advantages:
  • Useful for printers, servers, and other networking devices that need to be accessible to clients on the network.
  • If hosts normally access a server at a particular IPv4 address, it would not be good if that address changed, static assignment prevents that.
Static assignment of addressing information can provide increased control of network resources, but it can be time consuming to enter the information on each host. Static addresses also come with some drawbacks:
  • When IPv4 addresses are entered statically, the host only performs basic error checks on the address, so errors are more likely to occur.
  • It is important to maintain an accurate list of which IPv4 addresses are assigned to which devices, since these are permanent addresses that are not normally reused.
While static addressing provides control, it is time-consuming and error-prone for large or changing networks. This is where dynamic addressing with DHCP becomes the preferred method.
### Dynamic Assignment (DHCP)
On local networks it is often the case that the user population changes frequently. New users arrive with laptops and need a connection. Others have new workstations that need to be connected. Rather than have the network administrator assign IPv4 addresses for each workstation, it is easier to have IPv4 addresses assigned automatically. This is done using a protocol known as Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP). DHCP is generally the preferred method of assigning IPv4 addresses to hosts on large networks, because it reduces the burden on network support staff and virtually eliminates entry errors. Another benefit of DHCP is that an address is not permanently assigned to a host, but is only leased for a period of time. If the host is powered down or taken off the network, the address is returned to the pool for reuse. This is especially helpful with mobile users that come and go on a network. DHCP automatically assigns addressing information, such as:
  • IPv4 addresses
  • subnet masks
  • default gateways
  • other configuration information
#### Example
If you enter an airport or coffee shop with a wireless hotspot, DHCP makes it possible for you to access the internet.
1. Entering the Area
  • Your laptop's DHCP client contacts the local DHCP server via a wireless connection.
2. Receiving an Address
  • The DHCP server assigns an IPv4 address to your laptop.
  • With home networks, the DHCP server may be located at the ISP, with a host on the home network receiving its IPv4 configuration directly from the ISP.
  • Various types of devices can be DHCP servers, as long as they are running DHCP service software.
  • With most medium to large networks, the DHCP server is usually a local dedicated PC-based server.
#### How it works
Many home and small businesses networks use a wireless router and modem. In this case, the wireless router is both a DHCP client and a server.
1. Acting as a Client
  • The wireless router acts as a client to receive its IPv4 configuration from the ISP.
  • It receives the public IPv4 address from the ISP.
2. Acting as a Server
  • The router then acts as a DHCP server for internal hosts on the local network.
  • In this role, it distributes private addresses to internal hosts.
In addition to PC-based servers and wireless routers, other types of networking devices such as dedicated routers can provide DHCP services to clients, although this is not as common. ## DHCPv4 Operation
DHCP operates using a series of messages between the client and server. The process starts when a client needs an IP address:
1. The client sends a DHCP Discover packet, a broadcast message looking for any DHCP server on the network. It includes the client's MAC address.
  • Source IP: 0.0.0.0 (the client has no IP yet).
  • Destination IP: 255.255.255.255 (the limited broadcast address, which reaches all devices on the local subnet).
  • It includes the client's MAC address (in the CHADDR field) and is sent over UDP ports 67/68 (RFC 2131).
  • It is a limited broadcast (reaches all devices on the local subnet; routers do not forward it unless DHCP relay is configured).
  • The client may set the broadcast flag (the leftmost bit in the 16-bit flags field) to 1 to request that the server reply via broadcast instead of unicast (useful if the client's IP stack cannot yet accept unicast packets before full configuration; per RFC 2131 §4.1).
2. The DHCP server responds with a DHCP Offer packet, offering an available IP address, subnet mask, default gateway, and other settings. 3. The client sends a DHCP Request packet, accepting the offered address. 4. The server sends a DHCP Acknowledge (ACK) packet, confirming the lease and placing the IP address in its table associated with the client's MAC address.
This is commonly summarized as the DORA process (Discover, Offer, Request, Acknowledge). ### DHCPv4 Configuration Overview
DHCPv4 configuration involves setting up address pools, lease times, default gateway, DNS servers, and other options on the server. Common tasks include:
  • Defining a range of addresses to hand out
  • Setting lease duration
  • Configuring options
Packet Tracer labs often demonstrate configuring DHCP on a wireless router, where the router acts as both client (to ISP) and server (to internal hosts). Detailed DHCPv4 configuration on routers is covered in Advanced → NAT & PAT.
See Advanced for full details:
Advanced → NAT & PAT → Concepts & Types
## What DHCP Provides
When a device receives configuration from DHCP, it typically receives:
  • an IP address
  • a subnet mask
  • a default gateway
  • one or more DNS server addresses
See:
Internet Layer → Subnetting & Gateways → Subnet Masks & CIDR
## Why DHCP Is Important
Without DHCP:
  • Every device would need manual IP configuration
  • Address conflicts could occur more easily
  • Managing large networks would become complex
DHCP simplifies network administration and reduces configuration errors.
## DHCP Lease Concept
1. Lease Assigned
  • IP addresses assigned by DHCP are typically leased for a period of time.
2. Renewal
  • Before the lease expires, the client attempts to renew the lease to continue using the same IP address.
3. Expiration
  • If the lease is not renewed, the address may return to the available pool.
## DHCP and Private Networks
In most home and small office networks:
  • The router acts as the DHCP server
  • Clients automatically receive private IP addresses (e.g., 192.168.x.x)
See:
Foundations → Devices & Models → Routers → Roles & Types

# IP Addressing - Fundamentals - Introduction
The Internet Layer (Layer 3) is responsible for addressing and routing data across networks. It ensures packets can travel from a source device to a destination device, even when those devices are on different networks.
### Layer 3 Header
The Layer 3 header contains addressing information that enables routing. It includes: - Source Address — where the packet originates - Destination Address — where the packet is going These addresses allow routers to determine how to forward packets between networks.
### What IP Addressing Does
IP addressing allows devices to be uniquely identified on a network. Every device communicating on an IP network requires an IP address.
### Addressing Analogies
IP addresses can be compared to mailing addresses: - Network Address — like a street name, identifying the general location - Host Address — like a house number, identifying the specific device Example IPv4 address: - 10.10.0.50 - Network portion: 10.10.0 - Host portion: .50
### IPv4 and IPv6 Overview
Two primary IP versions exist: - IPv4 — Most common format: xxx.xxx.xxx.xxx (e.g., 192.168.1.1) - IPv6 — Uses eight groups of hexadecimal numbers
