Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/Internet/Ba.bodyMd:
# How ipconfig Shows and Fixes Your IP Configuration
### Why IP Configuration Matters
When a device does not receive an IP address, or has an incorrect IP configuration, it cannot communicate on the network or reach the internet. If the host also does not know the location of its DNS servers, it cannot translate domain names into IP addresses either. On Windows devices, the ipconfig command displays this configuration information directly from the command prompt. It has several useful options, including /all, /release, and /renew.
### Viewing Basic Configuration (ipconfig)
Running ipconfig alone displays the basic configuration for a host. This includes the IPv4 address, subnet mask, and default gateway. ```shell C:\> ipconfig Windows IP Configuration Ethernet adapter Ethernet: Media State . . . . . . . . . . . : Media disconnected Connection-specific DNS Suffix . : Wireless LAN adapter Wi-Fi: Connection-specific DNS Suffix . : lan Link-local IPv6 Address . . . . . : fe80::a1cc:4239:d3ab:2675%6 IPv4 Address. . . . . . . . . . . : 10.10.10.130 Subnet Mask . . . . . . . . . . . : 255.255.255.0 Default Gateway . . . . . . . . . : 10.10.10.1 C:\> ```
### Getting Full Details (ipconfig /all)
The command ipconfig /all displays additional information beyond the basic view. This includes the MAC address, the DNS server addresses, whether DHCP is enabled, the DHCP server address, and lease information. How does this help troubleshooting? Without a proper IP configuration, a host cannot participate in communication on a network. If it does not know where its DNS servers are, it cannot resolve names into IP addresses. ```shell C:\> ipconfig /all Windows IP Configuration Host Name . . . . . . . . . . . . : your-a9270112e3 Primary Dns Suffix . . . . . . . : Node Type . . . . . . . . . . . . : Hybrid IP Routing Enabled. . . . . . . . : No WINS Proxy Enabled. . . . . . . . : No DNS Suffix Search List. . . . . . : lan Ethernet adapter Ethernet: Media State . . . . . . . . . . . : Media disconnected Connection-specific DNS Suffix . : Description . . . . . . . . . . . : Realtek PCIe GBE Family Controller Physical Address. . . . . . . . . : 00-16-D4-02-5A-EC DHCP Enabled. . . . . . . . . . . : Yes Autoconfiguration Enabled . . . . : Yes Wireless LAN adapter Wi-Fi: Connection-specific DNS Suffix . : lan Description . . . . . . . . . . . : Intel(R) Dual Band Wireless-AC 3165 Physical Address. . . . . . . . . : 00-13-02-47-8C-6A DHCP Enabled. . . . . . . . . . . : Yes Autoconfiguration Enabled . . . . : Yes Link-local IPv6 Address . . . . . : fe80::a1cc:4239:d3ab:2675%6(Preferred) IPv4 Address. . . . . . . . . . . : 10.10.10.130(Preferred) Subnet Mask . . . . . . . . . . . : 255.255.255.0 Lease Obtained. . . . . . . . . . : Wednesday, September 2, 2020 10:03:43 PM Lease Expires . . . . . . . . . . : Friday, September 11, 2020 10:23:36 AM Default Gateway . . . . . . . . . : 10.10.10.1 DHCP Server . . . . . . . . . . . : 10.10.10.1 DHCPv6 IAID . . . . . . . . . . . : 98604135 DHCPv6 Client DUID. . . . . . . . : 00-01-00-01-1E-21-A5-84-44-A8-42-FC-0D-6F DNS Servers . . . . . . . . . . . : 10.10.10.1 NetBIOS over Tcpip. . . . . . . . : Enabled C:\> ```
### Releasing and Renewing (ipconfig /release and ipconfig /renew)
If IP addressing is assigned dynamically, ipconfig /release releases the current DHCP bindings. ipconfig /renew then requests fresh configuration information from the DHCP server. A host with faulty or outdated configuration information often only needs a renewal to regain connectivity. If a host cannot obtain fresh information after releasing, the diagnostic path is:
  • Verify the NIC has an illuminated link light, confirming a physical connection to the network.
  • If the link light is fine, the issue may be with the DHCP server itself.
  • If the DHCP server is reachable elsewhere, the issue may be with the network path leading to it.
```shell C:\> ipconfig /release Windows IP Configuration No operation can be performed on Ethernet while it has its media disconnected. Ethernet adapter Ethernet: Media State . . . . . . . . . . . : Media disconnected Connection-specific DNS Suffix . : Wireless LAN adapter Wi-Fi: Connection-specific DNS Suffix . : Link-local IPv6 Address . . . . . : fe80::a1cc:4239:d3ab:2675%6 Default Gateway . . . . . . . . . : ``` ```shell C:\> ipconfig /renew Windows IP Configuration No operation can be performed on Ethernet while it has its media disconnected. Ethernet adapter Ethernet: Media State . . . . . . . . . . . : Media disconnected Connection-specific DNS Suffix . : Wireless LAN adapter Wi-Fi: Connection-specific DNS Suffix . : lan Link-local IPv6 Address . . . . . : fe80::a1cc:4239:d3ab:2675%6 IPv4 Address. . . . . . . . . . . : 10.10.10.130 Subnet Mask . . . . . . . . . . . : 255.255.255.0 Default Gateway . . . . . . . . . : 10.10.10.1 C:\> ```
### Summary
  • ipconfig shows basic configuration: IPv4 address, subnet mask, default gateway.
  • ipconfig /all adds MAC address, DNS servers, DHCP status, and lease details.
  • ipconfig /release and ipconfig /renew clear and reacquire DHCP-assigned settings.
  • A failed renewal points troubleshooting toward the physical link, then the DHCP server, then the path to it.

Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/Internet/Ba.title: PingAndTraceroute
Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/Internet/Ba.bodyMd:
# Ping & Traceroute
### What ping Does
The ping command tests network connectivity. It sends ICMP Echo Requests to a destination and waits for a reply. If replies are received, the destination is reachable. ping is probably the most commonly used network utility, and most IP-enabled devices support some form of it. The command can be followed by either an IP address or the name of a destination host. When a ping is sent to an IP address, an echo request packet is sent across the network to that address. If the destination host receives it, it responds with an echo reply packet, and connectivity is verified. The ping is not successful if a message such as request timed out or general failure appears instead. If a ping is sent to a name rather than an IP address, a packet is first sent to a DNS server to resolve the name to an IP address. Once the IP address is obtained, the echo request is forwarded and the process proceeds as normal. If a ping to the IP address succeeds but a ping to the name does not, the problem is most likely with DNS.
### Example: Pinging a Gateway and a Domain
```shell C:\> ping 10.10.10.1 Pinging 10.10.10.1 with 32 bytes of data: Reply from 10.10.10.1: bytes=32 time=1ms TTL=64 Reply from 10.10.10.1: bytes=32 time=1ms TTL=64 Reply from 10.10.10.1: bytes=32 time=1ms TTL=64 Reply from 10.10.10.1: bytes=32 time=1ms TTL=64 Ping statistics for 10.10.10.1: Packets: Sent = 4, Received = 4, Lost = 0 (0% loss), Approximate round trip times in milli-seconds: Minimum = 1ms, Maximum = 1ms, Average = 1ms ``` ```shell C:\> ping www.cisco.com Pinging e2867.dsca.akamaiedge.net [104.112.72.241] with 32 bytes of data: Reply from 104.112.72.241: bytes=32 time=25ms TTL=53 Reply from 104.112.72.241: bytes=32 time=25ms TTL=53 Reply from 104.112.72.241: bytes=32 time=27ms TTL=53 Reply from 104.112.72.241: bytes=32 time=24ms TTL=53 Ping statistics for 104.112.72.241: Packets: Sent = 4, Received = 4, Lost = 0 (0% loss), Approximate round trip times in milli-seconds: Minimum = 24ms, Maximum = 27ms, Average = 25ms C:\> ``` The first example pings a default gateway directly by IP address, a fast, local reply at 1ms. The second pings a domain name, which resolves through DNS to 104.112.72.241 before the echo requests are sent, resulting in a longer, more realistic round trip of 24 to 27ms.
### Reading Ping Results
If pings to both the name and the IP address succeed, but the application still cannot be reached, the problem most likely resides in the application on the destination host, for example, the requested service may not be running. If neither ping succeeds, network connectivity along the path to the destination is most likely the problem. In this case, it is common practice to ping the default gateway.
  • If the ping to the default gateway succeeds, the problem is not local.
  • If the ping to the default gateway fails, the problem resides on the local network.
A ping can also fail even when network connectivity is not the actual problem. This can happen due to a firewall on the sending or receiving device, or a router along the path that is blocking ping traffic.
### ping Options
The basic ping command usually sends four echo requests and waits for a reply to each one. It can be modified with additional options to increase its usefulness.
Option Description
-t Pings the specified host until stopped.
-a Resolves addresses to hostnames.
-n count Sets the number of echo requests to send.
-l size Sets the send buffer size.
-f Sets the Don't Fragment flag in the packet (IPv4 only).
-i TTL Sets the Time To Live.
-w timeout Sets the timeout, in milliseconds, to wait for each reply.
-4 Forces the use of IPv4.
-6 Forces the use of IPv6.
### Traceroute (Path Visibility)
Traceroute shows the hop-by-hop path packets take to reach a destination. This helps identify where latency or packet loss may be occurring along the route. Common commands: #### Windows ```shell tracert google.com ``` #### macOS/Linux ```shell traceroute google.com ```

Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/Application.title: SSH
Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/Application.bodyMd:
# What Is SSH?
SSH stands for Secure Shell, a protocol that allows you to remotely access another device, such as a server, as if you were physically sitting at that device typing commands directly.
  • SSH is a more secure version of an older protocol, Telnet, which serves the same basic purpose.
  • Once connected, commands typed locally are executed on the remote device, regardless of physical distance.
See:
DevScriptStax → Git → Advanced → Remote Platforms → SSH Authentication
## What Is Telnet?
Before networks existed, text-based systems were often just display terminals physically attached to a central computer. Once networks became available, people needed a way to remotely access those systems the same way they did with a directly-attached terminal. Telnet was developed to meet that need.
  • Telnet dates back to the early 1970s, making it one of the oldest application-layer protocols in the TCP/IP suite.
  • It provides a standard method of emulating text-based terminal devices over a network.
  • Both the protocol itself and the client software that implements it are commonly referred to as Telnet.
  • Telnet servers listen for client requests on TCP port 23.
A connection using Telnet is called a virtual terminal (vty) session. Rather than using a physical device to connect to the server, Telnet uses software to create a virtual device that provides the same access to a server's command line interface (CLI) that a physical terminal would. Once connected, the client is able to execute commands as if it were locally connected to the server.
## Security Issues with Telnet
After a Telnet connection is established, a user can perform any authorized function on the server, just as if they were using a command line session on the server itself, starting and stopping processes, configuring the device, or even shutting it down. Although Telnet can require a login, it does not support encrypted data. All data exchanged during a Telnet session is transported as plaintext, meaning it can be easily intercepted and read. SSH offers an alternate, secure method for server access. It provides the structure for secure remote login, stronger authentication than Telnet, and supports transporting session data using encryption.
  • With Telnet, data intercepted by an attacker is clearly readable, including usernames and passwords.
  • With SSH, data intercepted by an attacker is encrypted and unreadable.
As a best practice, SSH should always be used in place of Telnet whenever possible.
## Connecting with a Terminal Emulator
Terminal emulator software, such as Tera Term, provides a client for connecting to a remote device using either Telnet or SSH.
1. Entering the Host
  • The host can be entered as either a host name or an IP address.
2. Selecting the Protocol
  • SSH is selected instead of Telnet for a secure connection.
3. Authenticating
  • A username and password are entered to authenticate the connection.
4. Remote Access Established
  • Once authenticated, the terminal behaves as if you were sitting directly at the remote device, entering commands there instead of locally.

Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/Transport/B.bodyMd:
# Port Ranges
Port numbers range from 0 to 65,535 and are divided into three categories by IANA (Internet Assigned Numbers Authority). These ranges help standardize port usage and prevent conflicts.
### Well-Known Ports
Ports 0–1023 are reserved for common, standardized services and applications.
  • Assigned to widely used protocols and services (e.g., HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS, TFTP).
  • Typically used as destination ports by servers.
  • Require elevated privileges to bind on most operating systems.
### Registered Ports
Ports 1024–49151 are registered for specific applications and services.
  • Assigned by IANA to organizations or vendors for proprietary or specialized applications.
  • Can be used as destination ports for registered services or as source ports by clients.
### Private / Dynamic / Ephemeral Ports
Ports 49152–65535 are private or dynamic ports.
  • Used as source ports by clients for outgoing connections.
  • Dynamically and randomly assigned by the operating system for each session.
  • Not registered or reserved — available for temporary use.
This range ensures clients can open multiple simultaneous connections without port conflicts.
See also:
Ports and Sockets (well-known port examples and multiplexing)

Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/Transport/B.title: PortsAndSockets
Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/Transport/B.bodyMd:
# Ports and Sockets
Port numbers are 16-bit values used at the Transport layer to identify specific applications or services on a host. They enable multiplexing, allowing multiple applications to communicate simultaneously over the same IP address.
### Server Ports (Well-Known Ports)
Servers providing standard services listen on fixed, well-known port numbers below 1024. These ports are assigned by convention and automatically used by clients.
Application / Service Port Number
HTTP (Hypertext Transfer Protocol) 80
HTTPS (HTTP Secure) 443
FTP (File Transfer Protocol) - Control 21
FTP - Data 20
SSH (Secure Shell) 22
Telnet 23
SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol) 25
DNS (Domain Name System) 53 (UDP/TCP)
DHCP (Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol) - Server 67 (UDP)
DHCP - Client 68 (UDP)
TFTP (Trivial File Transfer Protocol) 69 (UDP)
POP3 (Post Office Protocol version 3) 110 (TCP)
IMAP (Internet Message Access Protocol) 143 (TCP)
SNMP (Simple Network Management Protocol) 161 (UDP)
Clients (e.g., web browser) do not need to specify the port when accessing these services — the client software knows the standard port for the protocol (e.g., typing a URL assumes port 80 for HTTP). The server maintains a listening buffer for incoming requests addressed to its IP and the service port.
### Client Ports (Ephemeral Ports)
Clients use dynamically assigned source ports from the range above 1024 (ephemeral or temporary ports). These are randomly selected for each connection.
  • Example: A web browser requesting a page might use source port 5305 (random) → destination port 80 on the server.
  • The server responds with source port 80 → destination port 5305 (the browser knows this is the reply to its request).
  • Example: An FTP client might use source port 1305 (random) → destination port 21 on the server.
This allows multiple simultaneous connections from the same client (e.g., web browser + FTP client running at the same time, each with a different source port).
### Socket Pairs and Multiplexing
The combination of source IP address and source port number, or destination IP address and destination port number, is known as a **socket**. A socket pair uniquely identifies each connection (e.g., client 192.168.1.5:1099 → server 192.168.1.7:80 for web; client 192.168.1.5:1305 → server 192.168.1.7:21 for FTP). Sockets enable:
  • Multiple processes on a client to distinguish themselves from each other.
  • Multiple connections to a server process to be distinguished from each other.
  • The source port to act as a return address for the requesting application.
The Transport layer tracks these ports to ensure responses are forwarded to the correct application. Port numbers allow a single host to run many applications at once:
  • Multiple services on a server (web on 80, FTP on 21) listen independently.
  • Clients use unique source ports for each session.
  • The Transport layer uses source IP/port + destination IP/port (socket pair) to route data to the correct application.
This multiplexing/demultiplexing process is fundamental to modern networking.
### netstat Command
Unexplained TCP connections can pose a major security threat, as they may indicate unauthorized access or processes connected to the local host. The netstat command is an important network utility used to verify active TCP connections and open ports. Common usage:
  • By default, netstat displays protocols in use, local address and port, foreign address and port, and connection state (e.g., ESTABLISHED).
  • It attempts to resolve IP addresses to domain names and port numbers to well-known applications.
  • Use the -n option to display IP addresses and port numbers in numerical form (no resolution).
Example output (abbreviated):
  • Active Connections
  • Proto Local Address Foreign Address State
  • TCP 192.168.1.124:3126 192.168.0.2:netbios-ssn ESTABLISHED
  • TCP 192.168.1.124:3158 207.138.126.152:http ESTABLISHED
netstat helps troubleshoot connectivity, identify listening services, and detect unexpected connections.
See also:
Port Ranges (well-known, registered, and dynamic port categories)
Process Demultiplexing and Socket Pairs (advanced details)

Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/Transport/B.title: Introduction
Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/Transport/B.bodyMd:
# Transport Layer (Layer 4)
The Transport layer is responsible for end-to-end communication between applications running on different hosts. It receives data from the Application layer, segments it into smaller units, and passes it to the Internet layer for routing across networks. At the receiving end, it reassembles the segments and delivers them to the correct application. This layer provides two key services:
  • Multiplexing / Demultiplexing — Uses port numbers to identify the correct source and destination applications on the same host.
  • Delivery Control — Determines whether communication should be reliable (error-checked and ordered) or fast (low-overhead, best-effort).
### Primary Transport Protocols
Two protocols operate at this layer:
  • TCP
  • UDP
The choice of protocol depends on the application's needs: critical data integrity versus low latency and minimal overhead.
### Key Concepts at the Transport Layer
  • Segmentation and Reassembly — Breaks large application data into smaller segments and reassembles them at the destination.
  • Port Numbers — Allow multiple applications to use the network simultaneously by identifying conversations.
  • Connection Management — Some protocols establish connections before data transfer, while others send data immediately.
These mechanisms enable efficient and flexible communication across diverse network applications.
See:
TCP vs UDP (detailed comparison of the two protocols)
Ports & Sockets (how port numbers enable multiplexing)
Reliable Delivery and Flow Control (TCP-specific mechanisms)
See Glossary for definitions:
TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)
UDP (User Datagram Protocol)

Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/Transport/B.title: TCPvsUDP
Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/Transport/B.bodyMd:
# TCP vs UDP
Each protocol serves different needs based on reliability, speed, and overhead requirements.
### UDP (User Datagram Protocol)
UDP is a lightweight, connectionless protocol designed for speed and minimal overhead.
  • Used primarily for real-time or streaming communications (e.g., voice calls, video streaming).
  • No acknowledgments, sequencing, or retransmissions — dropped packets are not recovered.
  • Low overhead makes it ideal when speed matters more than perfect delivery.
  • Applications tolerate occasional packet loss (e.g., a dropped audio sample in a phone call is usually unnoticeable).
UDP segments data and adds only source and destination port numbers to identify conversations.
### TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)
TCP is a reliable, connection-oriented protocol that ensures accurate and ordered delivery.
  • Used when data integrity is critical (e.g., file transfers, web pages, bank transactions).
  • Includes sequencing numbers so the receiver can reorder packets and detect missing ones.
  • Provides acknowledgments — receiver confirms receipt, sender retransmits lost packets.
  • Uses windowing and flow control to adjust transmission rate based on network conditions (smaller windows in unreliable links, larger in reliable ones).
  • Higher overhead due to reliability mechanisms (handshakes, ACKs, retransmissions).
TCP guarantees delivery and order, making it suitable for applications where losing even one packet would be problematic.
### Comparison: TCP vs UDP
Feature TCP
Connection Connection-oriented (handshake)
Reliability Reliable (ACKs, retransmissions)
Ordering Yes (sequence numbers)
Flow Control Yes (windowing)
Overhead Higher
Use Cases Web, email, file transfer
Packet Loss Handling Retransmits lost packets
Feature UDP
Connection Connectionless
Reliability Unreliable (no ACKs)
Ordering No
Flow Control No
Overhead Lower
Use Cases Streaming, voice, DNS, DHCP
Packet Loss Handling Tolerates loss
The choice between TCP and UDP depends on the application's requirements: reliability versus speed.
See also:
Ports & Sockets (how ports identify conversations)
Reliable Delivery and Flow Control (TCP mechanisms in detail)

Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/Internet/Ba.title: Fundamentals
Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/Internet/Ba.bodyMd:
# IPv6 Fundamentals
### Why IPv6?
IPv6 (Internet Protocol version 6) is the successor to IPv4, designed to solve the exhaustion of IPv4 address space while adding improvements in efficiency, security, and autoconfiguration. With 128-bit addresses, IPv6 provides an enormous address space (approximately 340 undecillion addresses) — enough for every device on Earth and the growing Internet of Things (IoT). The long-term goal of IPv6 is native end-to-end communication from source to destination without translation. Tunneling and translation mechanisms (e.g., NAT64) are only temporary tools to help IPv6 coexist with IPv4 during the transition. They should be used only where necessary.
See: IPv4 Exhaustion & Transition
### IPv6 Address Representation
IPv6 addresses are 128 bits long and written as a string of hexadecimal values. IPv6 addresses are not case-sensitive and can be written in either lowercase or uppercase. Every four bits is represented by a single hexadecimal digit, for a total of 32 hexadecimal values. Each group of four hexadecimal digits is 16 bits and is sometimes called a **hextet**. The preferred format for writing an IPv6 address is x:x:x:x:x:x:x:x, with each “x” consisting of four hexadecimal values. Example of preferred format: ```shell 2001:0db8:acad:a088:0000:0000:0000:0123 ``` ### Hexadecimal Number System
Hexadecimal (base-16) uses digits 0–9 and letters A–F to represent values 0–15. Each hextet (four hex digits) represents 16 bits, making hexadecimal much shorter than writing 128 bits in binary. **Hex to Binary Conversion (0–7)**
Hex Binary
0 0000
1 0001
2 0010
3 0011
4 0100
5 0101
6 0110
7 0111
**Hex to Binary Conversion (8–F)**
Hex Binary
8 1000
9 1001
a 1010
b 1011
c 1100
d 1101
e 1110
f 1111
### IPv6 Formatting Rules
IPv6 addresses can be shortened using two main rules:
  • Rule 1: Omit leading zeros in any hextet (e.g., 0000 becomes 0, 0db8 becomes db8). Trailing zeros are not omitted.
    • Otherwise the address would become ambiguous (e.g., "abc" could mean "0abc" or "abc0").
  • Rule 2: Double colon (::) — replace one contiguous string of one or more all-zero hextets with :: (can only be used once per address).
**Rule 2 Details**: No matter how many consecutive hextets are all zeros (2, 3, 4, or more), they are always replaced by a single "::". There are never more than two colons together. Best practice: If there are multiple contiguous strings of all-zero hextets, use :: on the longest string. If equal, use it on the first string. Examples:
  • Preferred: 2001:0db8:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0001 → Shortened: 2001:db8::1
  • Preferred: 2001:0db8:0000:0001:0000:0000:0000:0001 → Shortened: 2001:db8:0:1::1
  • Preferred: 2001:0db8:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0001 → Shortened: 2001:db8::1
**Incorrect use example** (using :: twice creates ambiguity): shell 2001:db8:abcd:1234 (incorrect double ::) Possible expansions (ambiguous):
  • 2001:db8::abcd:0000:0000:1234
  • 2001:db8:abcd:0000:0000::1234
  • 2001::abcd:0000:0000:0000:1234
  • 2001:db8:abcd::0000:0000:1234
### Activity - IPv6 Address Representations
Practice converting IPv6 addresses between preferred (full) and shortened forms using the two rules.
  • Full: 2001:0db8:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0001 → Shortened: 2001:db8::1
  • Full: 2001:0db8:0000:0001:0000:0000:0000:0001 → Shortened: 2001:db8:0:1::1
See Glossary for full details: IPv6 Addressing → Hexadecimal Notation / Preferred Format

Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/Internet/Ba.title: AddressStructure
Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/Internet/Ba.bodyMd:
# IPv4 Address Structure
### Purpose of an IPv4 Address
A host needs an IPv4 address to participate on the internet and almost all LANs today. The IPv4 address is a logical network address that identifies a particular host. It must be properly configured and unique within the LAN, for local communication. It must also be properly configured and unique in the world, for remote communication. This is how a host is able to communicate with other devices on the internet. An IPv4 address is assigned to the network interface connection for a host. This connection is usually a network interface card (NIC) installed in the device. Examples of end-user devices with network interfaces include workstations, servers, network printers, and IP phones. Some servers can have more than one NIC and each of these has its own IPv4 address. Router interfaces that provide connections to an IP network will also have an IPv4 address. Every packet sent across the internet has a source and destination IPv4 address. This information is required by networking devices to ensure the information gets to the destination and any replies are returned to the source.
### IPv4 Format (Octets)
IPv4 (Internet Protocol version 4) is the most widely used IP addressing format. It uses a 32-bit address written as four decimal numbers (octets) separated by dots. Example: ```shell 192.168.1.10 ``` An IPv4 address is made of four octets:
  • Each octet ranges from 0 to 255
  • Four octets together form a 32-bit address
Example: ```shell 10.10.0.50 ``` Here is an IPv4 address in binary: ```shell 11010001101001011100100000000001 ``` Notice how difficult this address is to read. For this reason, the 32 bits are grouped into four 8-bit bytes called octets like this: ```shell 11010001.10100101.11001000.00000001 ``` Each octet is converted to its decimal value, separated by a decimal point or period. The above binary IPv4 becomes this dotted-decimal representation: ```shell 209.165.200.1 ```
### Network vs Host (Concept)
IPv4 addressing is typically split into two parts:
  • Network portion, identifies the network
  • Host portion, identifies the device on that network
The exact split between network and host portions is defined by the subnet mask or CIDR prefix. This is known as hierarchical addressing, routers only need to know how to reach networks, not every individual host.
See Glossary for full details: Glossary → IPv4 & IPv6 Addressing → Hierarchical Addressing
Multiple logical networks can exist on one physical network if hosts have different network portions. For example, three hosts on the same physical LAN might have 192.168.18.x addresses while three others have 192.168.5.x addresses. Hosts with the same network portion can communicate directly, but hosts with different network portions need routing. Another example of hierarchical addressing is the telephone system: the country code, area code, and exchange represent the network address, while the remaining digits represent a local phone number.
See: IP Addressing → Subnetting & Gateways → Subnet Masks & CIDR
### Verifying Your IP Address For the full ipconfig command reference, see the link below.
To check your local IP configuration: #### Windows ```shell ipconfig ``` #### Mac ```shell ifconfig ``` #### Linux/Ubuntu ```shell ip addr ``` or ```shell ifconfig ```
See: Internet Layer → Basics → ICMP & Commands → ipconfig (Viewing & Renewing IP Configuration)

Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/Internet/Ba.title: IPv4Exhaustion
Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/Internet/Ba.bodyMd:
# IPv4 Exhaustion & Transition
### IPv4 Address Depletion
IPv4 has a theoretical maximum of 4.3 billion addresses. Private addresses and NAT slowed depletion, but the growth of the Internet (WWW, mobile devices, IoT) has exhausted the available space. Note: The long-term solution to IPv4 address depletion is IPv6. ### Regional Internet Registries (RIRs)
Public IPv4 and IPv6 addresses are managed by the Internet Assigned Numbers Authority (IANA). IANA allocates blocks to five Regional Internet Registries (RIRs), who distribute them to ISPs and organizations. The five RIRs are:
  • ARIN (North America)
  • RIPE NCC (Europe, Middle East, Central Asia)
  • APNIC (Asia-Pacific)
  • AfriNIC (Africa)
  • LACNIC (Latin America and Caribbean)
Four of the five RIRs have already exhausted their IPv4 address pools (dates vary by region). ### Why Transition to IPv6?
IPv6 provides a 128-bit address space (340 undecillion addresses), eliminating exhaustion concerns. IPv6 also fixes IPv4 limitations and adds enhancements (e.g., improved ICMPv6 for autoconfiguration and address resolution). Mobile providers, ISPs, and content providers (YouTube, Netflix, Facebook) are leading the transition — many report >90% IPv6 traffic. Many companies are moving to IPv6-only internally. The Internet of Things (IoT) will require vastly more addresses — IPv6 is designed for this scale. ### Transition Methods
IPv4 and IPv6 will coexist for years. Migration techniques include:
  • Dual Stack — devices run both IPv4 and IPv6 stacks simultaneously (native IPv6 + IPv4 fallback).
  • Tunneling — encapsulate IPv6 packets inside IPv4 packets to cross IPv4 networks.
  • Translation — NAT64 translates between IPv6 and IPv4.
See Glossary for full details: IPv6 Addressing → Dual Stack / Tunneling / NAT64

Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/Internet/Ba.bodyMd:
# IPv4 vs IPv6 Comparison
IPv4 and IPv6 are two versions of the Internet Protocol used for logical addressing and routing. Both identify devices on networks, but they differ in structure, capacity, and design goals.
### Address Format
IPv4 IPv6
32-bit address 128-bit address
Dotted decimal format (e.g., 192.168.1.1) Hexadecimal format (e.g., 2001:0db8:85a3::7334)
~4.3 billion addresses Extremely large address space
### Address Exhaustion & Design Goals
IPv4 IPv6
Address space largely exhausted globally Designed to solve address exhaustion
Heavy reliance on NAT Designed for end-to-end connectivity
### Configuration Differences
IPv4 IPv6
Commonly uses DHCP Supports SLAAC (Stateless Autoconfiguration)
Broadcast supported No broadcast (uses multicast)
### Header & Efficiency
IPv4 IPv6
More optional header fields Simplified base header structure
Fragmentation handled by routers Fragmentation handled by sending host

Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/Internet/Ba.title: TransmissionTypes
Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/Internet/Ba.bodyMd:
# Types of Addresses
### Unicast Addresses
Unicast is one-to-one communication — one source device sends a packet to one destination device. A unicast packet uses:
  • A unicast destination IP address (single recipient)
  • A unicast source IP address (packet always originates from a single source)
This is true regardless of whether the destination is unicast, broadcast, or multicast — the source IP address is always unicast. Example: Printer ```shell Source: 172.16.4.1 Destination: 172.16.4.253 ``` Unicast is the normal method for most host-to-host traffic (web browsing, email, file transfer).
### Broadcast Addresses
Broadcast is one-to-all communication — a device sends a packet to every device on the local network. A broadcast packet has a destination IP address with all ones (1s) in the host portion (or 32 ones for the entire address). By default, routers do not forward broadcast packets — they stop at the broadcast domain. Note: IPv4 uses broadcast packets. IPv6 does not have broadcast (uses multicast instead). Broadcast traffic uses network resources and forces every receiving device to process the packet, so it should be limited to avoid performance issues. Subdividing networks (using routers) helps control broadcast traffic.
See broadcast domain details and segmentation:
Internet Layer → Basics → IP Addressing → Subnetting & Gateways → Broadcast Domains & Segmentation
Example: Limited Broadcast ```shell Source: 172.16.4.1 Destination: 255.255.255.255 ``` The switch floods the packet out all ports (except incoming), and every device on the network receives it.
### Multicast Addresses
Multicast is one-to-many communication — a single source sends a packet to a selected group of devices (multicast clients) that subscribe to a multicast group. IPv4 multicast range: 224.0.0.0 to 239.255.255.255. Benefits: reduces traffic (one packet instead of multiple unicast copies). Hosts subscribe to a group via a client program → process packets sent to the multicast address (plus their own unicast address). Common use: routing protocols like OSPF (uses 224.0.0.5). Only OSPF-enabled devices process these packets — others ignore them. Example: Multicast Group Address ```shell Source: 172.16.4.1 Destination: ``` Only subscribed devices (e.g., 172.16.4.3 and 172.16.4.4) process the packet.
Type Communication
Unicast One-to-one
Broadcast One-to-all on local network
Multicast One-to-many (group)
Destination Range/Example Forwarded by Routers?
Normal host addresses (e.g., 172.16.4.253) Yes
255.255.255.255 (limited) or network.255 (directed) No
224.0.0.0–239.255.255.255 Yes (if multicast routing enabled)
See Glossary for full details: IPv4 & IPv6 Addressing → Unicast / Broadcast / Multicast

Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/Internet/Ba.title: TypesOfAddresses
Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/Internet/Ba.bodyMd:
# Types of Addresses
### Public and Private IPv4 Addresses
IPv4 addresses fall into different categories based on their purpose and whether they can be routed across the public Internet. Public IPv4 addresses are globally unique and routable between ISP routers. They are required for any device that must be directly reachable from the Internet. Private IPv4 addresses (defined in RFC 1918) are reserved for internal networks and are not routable on the public Internet. They can be reused by any organization.
  • 10.0.0.0 – 10.255.255.255 (/8)
  • 172.16.0.0 – 172.31.255.255 (/12)
  • 192.168.0.0 – 192.168.255.255 (/16)
Most internal networks, from home setups to large enterprises, use private addresses for all devices (hosts and routers) within their intranet. Private addresses were introduced in the mid-1990s due to the rapid depletion of IPv4 address space caused by the growth of the World Wide Web. The long-term solution to IPv4 depletion is IPv6. ### Allocation of Public IPv4 Addresses
Public IPv4 addresses must be unique across the Internet. Both IPv4 and IPv6 addresses are managed by the Internet Assigned Numbers Authority (IANA). IANA allocates blocks of IP addresses to the five Regional Internet Registries (RIRs). RIRs are responsible for distributing IP addresses to Internet Service Providers (ISPs), who then provide IPv4 address blocks to organizations and smaller ISPs. Organizations can also obtain addresses directly from an RIR (subject to that RIR's policies). ### Routing to the Internet with Private Addresses
Devices using private IPv4 addresses cannot be directly reached from the Internet. Packets with a private source address and public destination must be translated before being forwarded to an ISP. Network Address Translation (NAT) is performed by the router that connects the internal network to the ISP. NAT translates the private source address to a public address for outbound traffic and tracks return replies to forward them to the correct internal device.
See Advanced for full NAT details: Advanced → NAT & PAT → Concepts & Types
### Special-Use IPv4 Addresses
Certain IPv4 addresses are reserved for special purposes and have restrictions or specific uses:
  • Loopback — 127.0.0.0/8 (commonly 127.0.0.1) — used by a host to send traffic to itself (e.g., testing IP configuration with ping 127.0.0.1).
  • APIPA (Automatic Private IP Addressing) — 169.254.0.0/16 — automatically assigned when a Windows client cannot obtain an address from DHCP.
  • Link-local — 169.254.0.0/16 — used for local communication when no DHCP server or manual configuration is available.
### Legacy Classful Addressing
In 1981, IPv4 addresses were assigned using classful addressing as defined in RFC 790 (Assigned Numbers). Customers were allocated a network address based on one of three main classes (A, B, or C), with fixed network/host divisions based on the first octet.
  • Class A — 0.0.0.0/8 to 127.0.0.0/8 — designed for extremely large networks with more than 16 million host addresses. Used a fixed /8 prefix; the first octet indicated the network, and the remaining three octets were for hosts.
  • Class B — 128.0.0.0/16 to 191.255.0.0/16 — designed for moderate to large networks with up to approximately 65,000 host addresses. Used a fixed /16 prefix; the first two octets indicated the network, and the remaining two octets were for hosts.
  • Class C — 192.0.0.0/24 to 223.255.255.0/24 — designed for small networks with a maximum of 254 hosts. Used a fixed /24 prefix; the first three octets indicated the network, and the remaining octet was for hosts.
Note: There was also a **Class D** multicast block (224.0.0.0 to 239.255.255.255) and a **Class E** experimental block (240.0.0.0 to 255.255.255.255). At the time, with a limited number of computers using the Internet, classful addressing was an effective way to allocate addresses. However, it wasted large amounts of address space — Class A and B networks had very large numbers of host addresses, while Class C had very few. Class A networks alone accounted for 50% of all IPv4 addresses, leaving most available addresses unused. In the mid-1990s, with the introduction and rapid growth of the World Wide Web (WWW), classful addressing was deprecated to more efficiently allocate the limited IPv4 address space. Classful address allocation was replaced with classless addressing (CIDR), which is used today. Classless addressing ignores the rules of classes (A, B, C). Public IPv4 network addresses (network addresses and subnet masks) are now allocated based on the number of addresses that can be justified. ### Assignment of IP Addresses
IPv4 addresses can be assigned in two main ways:
  • Static — manually configured on the device (common for servers, printers, routers, and devices that need a consistent address).
  • Dynamic — automatically assigned using DHCP (most common for end-user devices like PCs, phones, and laptops).
See Fundamentals for DHCP details: Fundamentals → DHCP (Address Assignment)
See Glossary for full details: IPv4 & IPv6 Addressing → Public / Private / Special-Use

Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/NetworkAcce.title: ARP
Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/NetworkAcce.bodyMd:
# Address Resolution Protocol (ARP)
## What Is ARP?
ARP maps IP addresses (Layer 3) to MAC addresses (Layer 2). When a device wants to send data to an IP on the same network, it uses ARP to find the matching MAC address.
## ARP Process
ARP resolves IP to MAC using a request-reply mechanism with broadcast containment.
1. Check ARP Cache
  • Device checks its ARP table for the destination IP's MAC entry.
2. Send ARP Request (if no entry)
  • Broadcast ARP request: "Who has 192.168.1.10? Tell 192.168.1.1".
  • Destination MAC: FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF (Ethernet broadcast).
  • Contained to broadcast domain.
3. Target Replies
  • Target device (with matching IP) sends unicast ARP reply with its MAC.
4. Cache and Use Entry
  • Sender adds entry to ARP table for future use.
  • Encapsulates data in frame with resolved MAC and sends.
See: Internet Layer → Basics → IP Addressing → Subnetting & Gateways → Broadcast Domains & Segmentation
### Broadcast Containment
ARP requests are broadcasts, but contained to the local broadcast domain to prevent flooding.
  • Broadcast domain: Set of devices that receive broadcasts (e.g., VLAN or subnet).
  • Switches forward broadcasts within domain; routers block them between domains.
  • Reduces traffic and prevents ARP storms in large networks.
## ARP Cache Viewing
Each OS has different ways to view the ARP table. For Windows/Linux/macOS: Show cached entries ```shell arp -a ``` For Windows/Linux/macOS: Show usage help ```shell arp ``` For Linux/Ubuntu: Show current table (alternative) ```shell ip neigh show ``` For Linux/Ubuntu: Show current table (numeric, no resolution) ```shell arp -n ```
### Static ARP Entries (Manual)
You can add or delete static entries. For Windows/macOS: Add entry ```shell arp -s ``` For Windows/macOS: Delete entry ```shell arp -d ``` For Linux: Add entry (alternative) ```shell ip neigh add lladdr dev ``` For Linux: Delete entry ```shell ip neigh del dev ```
## ARP vs DNS
Think of ARP as the Layer 2 version of DNS:
  • DNS: Hostname → IP
  • ARP: IP → MAC Address
Both are lookup systems that bridge identifiers to usable addresses.
## ARP Issues
  • Excessive broadcasts in large domains.
  • ARP spoofing/poisoning attacks (mitigated by dynamic ARP inspection).
### Summary
ARP is the behind-the-scenes translator that helps your machine say: “Okay, I know the IP — but how do I find the device?” It’s essential for local packet delivery and Layer 2 accuracy.
See related concepts:
Internet Layer → Basics → IP Addressing → Subnetting & Gateways → Broadcast Domains & Segmentation

Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/NetworkAcce.title: Deencapsulation
Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/NetworkAcce.bodyMd:
# De-encapsulation
### What Is De-encapsulation?
De-encapsulation is the reverse process of encapsulation. It occurs when data is received and must be reconstructed for the application.
  • Starts at the Physical Layer and moves upward.
  • Each layer removes its header/trailer, leaving the payload for the next layer.
  • Ends at the Application Layer, where the original data is presented.
### De-encapsulation Flow (Receive Path)
  • Physical Layer → receives raw bits, converts to frames
  • Data Link Layer → validates FCS, strips MAC info
  • Internet Layer → checks IP header, TTL, forwards payload
  • Transport Layer → reassembles segments, checks ports
  • Application Layer → presents usable data to the service
## De-encapsulation at Routers (Remote Networks)
For packets destined to remote networks, routers perform de-encapsulation and re-encapsulation.
1. Router receives frame on incoming interface
  • De-encapsulates Layer 2 frame (removes MAC header/trailer).
  • Examines Layer 3 IP packet (destination IP).
2. Determines next-hop
  • Uses routing table to find outgoing interface.
3. Re-encapsulates
  • Encapsulates IP packet in new Layer 2 frame for outgoing interface.
  • New source MAC: Router's outgoing interface MAC.
  • New destination MAC: Next-hop device's MAC (or final host if last hop).
4. Forwards frame
  • MAC addresses change hop-by-hop; IP addresses remain constant end-to-end.
Example (remote path):
Link Destination MAC
PC1 to R1 bb-bb-bb (R1 G0/0/0)
R1 to R2 dd-dd-dd (R2 G0/0/1)
R2 to PC2 55-55-55 (PC2)
Source IPv4: 192.168.10.10 (PC1) Destination IPv4: 10.1.1.10 (PC2) — unchanged.
See: Data Link - Frames & Addressing - MAC Addressing (hop-by-hop MAC change)
### Common Pitfalls in De-encapsulation
  • MTU mismatch → fragmentation or dropped packets.
  • Stale ARP cache → wrong MAC resolution.
  • Incorrect default gateway → packets never leave subnet.
  • Header corruption → fails checksum, dropped at layer.

Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/NetworkAcce.title: Encapsulation
Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/NetworkAcce.bodyMd:
# What Is Encapsulation? --- Encapsulation is the process of wrapping data with the protocol information required at each layer of the TCP/IP model. It occurs when data is sent from a client or host. - Begins at the Application Layer. - Passes through the Transport, Internet, Data Link, and Physical layers. - Each layer adds its own header (and sometimes trailer). - Ensures the data can be properly delivered and interpreted by the destination. --- ## Encapsulation Flow (Send Path)
🔹 Application Layer → user data (HTTP request, email, etc.) 🔹 Transport Layer → adds ports and reliability (TCP/UDP header) 🔹 Internet Layer → adds logical addressing (IP header) 🔹 Data Link Layer → adds physical addressing (MAC header + FCS) 🔹 Physical Layer → converts frame into bits on the medium
### Example: HTTP Request Encapsulation
App: HTTP GET /index.html L4: TCP src:51532 dst:80 SEQ=1000 ACK=0 Flags=SYN L3: IPv4 src:10.0.1.10 dst:93.184.216.34 TTL=64 L2: Eth dst:00:11:22:33:44:55 src:66:77:88:99:aa:bb type=0x0800 L1: Bits transmitted on the medium… --- ## OSI vs TCP/IP Mapping ---
### TCP/IP ↔ OSI equivalence
Layer (TCP/IP) Equivalent OSI Layer(s)
Application Application, Presentation, Session
Transport Transport
Internet Network
Network Access Data Link, Physical
### PDU names and common fields
Layer (TCP/IP) PDU Name + Key Fields
Application PDU: Data
Key fields: Protocol payload
Transport PDU: Segment
Key fields: Ports, SEQ/ACK
Internet PDU: Packet
Key fields: IP address, TTL
Network Access PDU: Frame / Bits
Key fields: MAC, FCS, encoding
---

Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/NetworkAcce.title: Introduction
Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/NetworkAcce.bodyMd:
# How Devices Talk on a Local Network
### What Is Layer 2?
Layer 2, also known as the **Data Link Layer**, defines how devices communicate over a **local network**. It packages data into frames and uses **MAC addresses** to deliver them to the correct device. It’s the layer responsible for **local delivery**, sitting just above the physical hardware (Layer 1).
### Key Functions of Layer 2
Layer 2 is responsible for: - **Framing:** Wrapping data into manageable chunks - **Addressing:** Using **MAC addresses** for sender/receiver ID - **Error detection:** Ensuring frames arrive correctly - **Flow control:** Regulating how fast devices exchange data These services allow local devices to communicate — no routers required.
### Where MAC Addresses Come In
Every device’s Network Interface Card (NIC) has a built-in MAC address (also called a BIA). Layer 2 uses these addresses to direct traffic between devices. For more details on MAC format, uniqueness, and NICs:
See: TCP/IP Model → Network Access → Data Link → Frames & Addressing → Burned-In Address (BIA)
TCP/IP Model → Network Access → Data Link → Frames & Addressing → MAC Addressing
### When ARP Enters the Picture
When a device only knows the IP address of its target, it uses ARP to determine the correct MAC address. This allows Layer 2 to forward the frame to the appropriate device on the local network. To explore ARP tables and command-line tools:
See: TCP/IP Model → Network Access → Data Link → Core Concepts → Address Resolution Protocol (ARP)
### Layer 2 Protocols
Layer 2 supports common protocols such as: - Ethernet (IEEE 802.3) — wired networks - Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11) — wireless networks These protocols define how devices frame and transmit data at the Data Link layer.
### Understanding Framing Through Analogy
Think of sending data across a network like mailing a letter. The message inside the envelope represents the payload — the actual data being delivered. The envelope represents the Layer 2 frame, which contains addressing information required for local delivery. Just as different countries may use different mailing formats or packaging requirements, wired and wireless networks use different frame formats: - Ethernet (802.3) for wired networks - Wi-Fi (802.11) for wireless networks The contents inside the frame may be identical, but the outer framing and addressing structure can differ depending on the medium. This process of placing one message format inside another is called encapsulation.
### Summary
Layer 2 defines the rules for **local communication**. It wraps data into frames, labels it with MAC addresses, and ensures reliable delivery across a local segment — all without requiring a router.

Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/Internet/Ba.title: BroadcastDomainsAndSegmentation
Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/Internet/Ba.bodyMd:
# Broadcast Domains & Segmentation
### Reasons to Divide Networks
As networks grow in size and complexity, dividing a single large local network into multiple smaller segments becomes necessary for several important reasons.
  • Control Broadcast Traffic: In a large switched network, every broadcast is flooded to all devices, forcing unnecessary processing and potential performance degradation. Smaller segments reduce the size of broadcast domains.
  • Improve Security: Isolating departments or functional groups prevents unauthorized access (e.g., sales department devices cannot reach accounting servers or printers, and network management tools remain protected).
  • Support Geographical and Organizational Separation: Different floors, buildings, or relocated departments may be physically too far apart for a single local network to serve efficiently.
Routers are the primary devices used to create this division. Each router interface connects to a separate network, establishing independent broadcast domains and distinct IP subnets. Traffic between these segments requires routing.
See also:
Routers → Roles & Types (router as gateway between networks)
Routers → Interfaces (each interface defines a separate network)
### What Is a Broadcast Domain?
A broadcast domain is a logical division of a network where all devices receive broadcast frames sent by any device in that domain.
  • Broadcasts are Ethernet-layer (Layer 2) messages sent to the broadcast MAC address (FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF).
  • Common examples include ARP requests and DHCP Discover messages.
  • Switches propagate (flood) broadcasts out all ports except the incoming port.
  • Routers do not forward broadcasts — they stop at the broadcast domain boundary.
This means every device in the same broadcast domain must process every broadcast frame, which can slow down the network if the domain is too large. ### How Devices Use Broadcasts
Devices on Ethernet LANs use broadcasts for discovery and configuration:
  • ARP (Address Resolution Protocol) sends a broadcast to discover the MAC address for a known IPv4 address on the local network.
  • DHCP Discover messages are sent as broadcasts to locate a DHCP server when a host needs an IP address.
Switches flood these broadcast frames throughout the broadcast domain, but routers stop them. ### Routers Segment Broadcast Domains
Routers do not propagate Ethernet broadcasts to other interfaces.
  • When a router receives a broadcast on one interface, it does not forward it out other interfaces.
  • Each router interface connects to its own separate broadcast domain.
  • Broadcasts from one LAN do not reach devices on another LAN connected through a router.
This is known as segmentation: routers separate Layer 2 broadcast domains, reducing unnecessary broadcast traffic and improving network performance. ### Problems with Large Broadcast Domains
A large broadcast domain connects many hosts and can generate excessive broadcasts.
  • Every device must accept and process each broadcast packet.
  • Excessive broadcasts slow down the network and individual devices.
  • Large domains increase the risk of broadcast storms (e.g., from misconfigurations, hardware/software problems, or malicious intent).
### Solution: Segment Networks with Subnetting
The solution is to reduce the size of networks by creating smaller broadcast domains in a process called subnetting.
  • Subnetting divides a large network into smaller subnets using longer prefix lengths.
  • Broadcasts are confined to smaller subnets, reducing overall network traffic.
  • Smaller domains improve performance, enable security policies (e.g., control which subnets can communicate), and limit the impact of abnormal broadcast traffic.
Subnetting is a fundamental skill for network administrators — various methods exist to help understand the process. ### Ways to Subnet Networks
Network administrators can create subnets using any logical division that makes sense for the network. Examples of subnetting strategies:
  • By location (e.g., different floors in a building)
  • By group or function (e.g., Administration, Human Resources, Students, Accounting)
  • By device type (e.g., all hosts, all servers, all printers)
In each case, subnets use longer prefix lengths to identify smaller networks.
See: IPv4 Subnetting
See Glossary for related terms: IPv4 & IPv6 Addressing → Broadcast

Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/Internet/Ba.title: DefaultGatewayAndRouting
Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/Internet/Ba.bodyMd:
# Default Gateway & Routing Tables
A default gateway is the router interface on a local network that forwards packets to destinations outside the local subnet. It serves as the exit point for traffic destined for remote networks or the Internet.
### Example
If a device has the IP address:
  • 192.168.1.10
Then the default gateway is typically:
  • 192.168.1.1
Packets addressed to remote destinations are sent to this gateway address.
### Local vs Remote Destination Determination
A host determines whether a destination is local or remote by comparing the network portions of the source and destination IP addresses.
1. **AND Operation on Source**
  • The host performs a binary AND operation between its own IP address and subnet mask.
2. **AND Operation on Destination**
  • It performs the same AND operation on the destination IP address and subnet mask.
3. **Compare Results**
  • If the results match → destination is local (direct Layer 2 delivery using ARP).
  • If the results differ → destination is remote (packet sent to default gateway).
See: Routers → Roles & Types (detailed local/remote ANDing and gateway operation)
### Role of the Default Gateway
When the destination is remote, the host:
  • Encapsulates the IP packet with the default gateway's MAC address as the destination MAC (obtained via ARP).
  • Sends the frame to the router interface.
  • The router receives the frame, de-encapsulates it, reads the destination IP, and forwards it toward the remote network.
See: De-encapsulation (router forwarding process: de-encap, routing decision, re-encap)
ARP (how host resolves gateway MAC)
### Basic Routing Table Function
The routing table is the router's map for deciding where to forward packets.
  • Contains entries with destination networks, subnet masks, next-hop addresses or exit interfaces, and administrative distance/metrics.
  • The router examines the destination IP address in the packet and performs a longest prefix match against the table.
  • If a match is found → packet is forwarded out the specified interface or to the next-hop router.
  • If no match → packet is dropped or sent to a default route (gateway of last resort) if configured.
### Basic Routing Table Function
The routing table is the router's map for deciding where to forward packets. Routing tables are not concerned with individual host addresses — they contain addresses of networks and the best path to reach those networks.
  • Entries can be added dynamically (from other routers) or statically (manually by administrator).
  • The router examines the destination IP address in the packet and performs a longest prefix match against the table.
  • If a match is found → packet is forwarded out the specified interface or to the next-hop router.
  • If no match → packet is dropped unless a default route is configured.
A default route (gateway of last resort) forwards packets with unknown destination networks. It is often the interface to another router or the Internet. Typical routing table entry fields include:
  • Type — Connection type (e.g., C for directly connected).
  • Network — Destination network address.
  • Port/Interface — Outgoing interface used to forward packets.
The routing table enables routers to connect multiple IP networks and direct traffic between them.
See: Routers → Interfaces (interface IP as default gateway for connected hosts)

Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/Internet/Ba.title: SubnetMasksAndCIDR
Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/Internet/Ba.bodyMd:
# Subnet Masks & CIDR
Subnetting determines how an IP address is divided between network and host portions.
### Subnet Mask
A Subnet Mask defines which part of an IP address represents the network and which part represents the host. Example: - 255.255.255.0 This mask indicates that the first three octets identify the network, and the last octet identifies the host.
### Network Portion in a Multi-Network Environment
In a network with multiple local area networks (such as different departments), each LAN has its own unique network portion.
For example:
- Sales Department: network portion 192.168.3.0 — all devices start with 192.168.3.x - Accounting Department: network portion 192.168.2.0 — devices start with 192.168.2.x - Network Management: network portion 192.168.1.0 — devices start with 192.168.1.x
All devices on the same local network must share the same network portion. The host portion must be unique within that network (e.g., no two devices can have 192.168.3.10). If a device is moved to a different network without updating its IP address, it will not be able to communicate — the network portion will not match the local network.
See also: IP Addressing → IPv4 → Address Structure (Network vs Host Concept)

Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/NetworkAcce.title: BurnedInAddress
Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/NetworkAcce.bodyMd:
## Burned-In Address (BIA) --- ### What Is a Burned-In Address? A **Burned-In Address (BIA)** is a permanent hardware identifier assigned to a device’s **Network Interface Card (NIC)**. It’s more commonly called a **MAC address** and is used by Layer 2 for device identification and local delivery. --- ### Other Names for the BIA You may hear it referred to as: - **MAC Address** - **Ethernet Address** - **Physical Address** - **Layer 2 Address** - **BIA (Burned-In Address)** They all mean the same thing — a unique Layer 2 address. --- ### Format and Bit Length - The BIA is **48 bits**, or **6 bytes** long - It’s written in **hexadecimal**, usually with colons or hyphens Example: 12:34:56:78:9A:BC Each pair represents 1 byte (8 bits) of the full address. --- ### NICs and Device Identification Every device has a **Network Interface Card (NIC)** — the component that connects it to a network. Each NIC is assigned a unique MAC address by the manufacturer, making it possible to identify devices on a local area network. This address never changes unless manually spoofed. --- ### Physical Connections Layer 2 operates over both: - **Wired connections** (Ethernet cables to a switch) - **Wireless connections** (Wi-Fi signals to an access point) Regardless of the medium, MAC addresses are required for local delivery. --- ### MAC Address Uniqueness Manufacturers are responsible for assigning **globally unique** MAC addresses. The first half of the address identifies the manufacturer (OUI), and the second half is a device-specific value. This helps prevent conflicts between devices on the same network. --- ### Real-World Analogy: Community Mailboxes Think of a: - **Neighborhood** with houses (IP addresses) - **Shared mailbox station** with labeled slots (MAC addresses) You drop a letter addressed to house .51, and the mailbox system maps it to the correct slot — like how ARP maps IPs to MACs and Layer 2 handles local delivery. --- ### Summary The Burned-In Address is the Layer 2 "name tag" for every networked device. It ensures reliable local delivery and is foundational to Ethernet and Wi-Fi networks. Without it, no device would know who’s who on the local segment. ---

Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/NetworkAcce.title: EthernetFrameFields
Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/NetworkAcce.bodyMd:
# Breaking Down the Ethernet Frame
### Overview of the Ethernet Frame
An Ethernet frame is the Layer 2 structure used to deliver data from one Network Interface Card (NIC) to another on the same local network. Each frame is divided into specific fields. These fields control synchronization, addressing, payload identification, and error detection. The numbers associated with each field represent the size in **bytes**. To convert bytes to bits, multiply by 8.
#### Example:
7 bytes × 8 = 56 bits
### Ethernet Frame Field Sizes
Field Size (Bytes)
Preamble 7
Start Frame Delimiter (SFD) 1
Destination MAC Address 6
Source MAC Address 6
Length / Type 2
Data (Payload) 46 – 1500
Frame Check Sequence (FCS) 4
### Preamble
The preamble is used to synchronize the receiving NIC with the incoming bit stream. It prepares the receiving device so it can properly interpret the bits that follow.
### Start Frame Delimiter (SFD)
The Start Frame Delimiter indicates that the actual Ethernet frame is about to begin. It signals to the receiving NIC that the next fields will contain meaningful frame information.
### Destination and Source MAC Addresses
The **Destination MAC Address** identifies the intended receiving device on the local network. The **Source MAC Address** identifies the device that originated the frame. Switches use these addresses to make forwarding decisions within a Layer 2 network.
### Length / Type Field
This 2-byte field can serve one of two purposes: - **Length** — Indicates the size of the payload (used in IEEE 802.3 framing). - **Type** — Identifies the protocol encapsulated inside the payload (used in Ethernet II framing). Examples of common type values include: - IPv4 - IPv6 This allows the receiving device to know what kind of data is being carried inside the frame.
### Data (Payload)
The data field contains the encapsulated Layer 3 packet. This may include: - An IPv4 packet - An IPv6 packet - A TCP segment - Application-layer data such as HTTP Ethernet itself does not interpret this data. It simply delivers the frame from one NIC to another. The payload must be between 46 and 1500 bytes. If it is smaller than 46 bytes, padding is added to meet the minimum frame size requirement.
### Frame Check Sequence (FCS)
The Frame Check Sequence provides error detection. The receiving device performs a calculation to verify that the frame was not corrupted during transmission. If the calculated value does not match the FCS value: - The frame is discarded. This mechanism helps ensure reliable communication at Layer 2. The FCS is calculated using a Cyclic Redundancy Check (CRC) algorithm.
### Understanding Frame Structure Through a Letter Analogy
To better understand how an Ethernet frame works, imagine sending a letter through the mail. Just like a physical letter must follow a specific format to be delivered correctly, an Ethernet frame must follow a structured format to be delivered across a network.
### Envelope vs Frame Header
Letter Element Ethernet Equivalent
Recipient address on envelope Destination MAC Address
Sender address on envelope Source MAC Address
Start of message indicator Start Frame Delimiter (SFD)
Content of letter Data (Payload)
End of message indicator Frame Check Sequence (FCS)
### Step-by-Step Analogy
1. The sender writes the message → This represents the payload. 2. The sender places the message inside an envelope → This represents encapsulation into a frame. 3. The envelope is labeled with the destination and sender addresses → These are the MAC addresses. 4. The postal system delivers the envelope → Switches forward the frame based on MAC addresses. 5. The recipient verifies the envelope and removes the letter → The receiving NIC checks the FCS and processes the payload. If the envelope is damaged or incorrectly addressed, the letter may not be delivered. Similarly, if a frame fails the FCS check or has incorrect addressing, it is discarded.
### Summary
An Ethernet frame contains multiple structured fields that allow: - Synchronization - Local addressing - Payload identification - Error detection Together, these fields enable reliable NIC-to-NIC communication on a local network.

Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/NetworkAcce.title: MACAddressing
Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/NetworkAcce.bodyMd:
# Identifying Devices with MAC Addresses
### What Is MAC Addressing?
MAC addressing is the mechanism Layer 2 uses to identify source and destination devices on a local network. MAC stands for Media Access Control, the sublayer of the Data Link layer responsible for physical addressing. Every frame includes:
  • A source MAC address
  • A destination MAC address
These addresses determine how switches forward frames within a local segment. Switches store learned MAC addresses in a hardware table:
See: Switching → Basics → Fundamentals → MAC Address Table
## MAC Address vs IP Address
MAC addresses are physical (Layer 2), assigned to hardware, and used for local network delivery. IP addresses are logical (Layer 3), assigned to devices, and used for end-to-end routing across networks.
  • MAC: Fixed (burned-in), 48-bit hexadecimal (e.g., 00:11:22:33:44:55).
  • IP: Configurable, 32-bit decimal for IPv4 (e.g., 192.168.1.10).
  • ARP bridges them for local Ethernet framing.
Both are required for complete communication across networks.
See: Internet Layer → Basics → IP Addressing → IPv4 → Address Structure
### MAC Address Usage in Frames
When a device sends data:
  • The source MAC identifies the sender
  • The destination MAC identifies the next hop
Switches examine MAC addresses to make forwarding decisions. They do not inspect IP addresses when operating at Layer 2.
### Local (Same Network) MAC Usage
For destinations on the same network, destination MAC = target device's MAC. Example (PC1 to PC2 on same subnet):
Destination MAC Source MAC
55-55-55 aa-aa-aa
Source IPv4: 192.168.10.10 Destination IPv4: 192.168.10.11
### Remote (Different Network) MAC Usage
For remote destinations, destination MAC = default gateway/router interface MAC. Routers de-encapsulate Layer 2, route based on IP, re-encapsulate with new MACs for outgoing interface. Example (PC1 to PC2 on remote network):
1. PC1 to R1
  • Destination MAC: bb-bb-bb (R1 G0/0/0)
2. R1 to R2
  • Destination MAC: dd-dd-dd (R2 G0/0/1)
3. R2 to PC2
  • Destination MAC: 55-55-55 (PC2)
MAC changes hop-by-hop; IP remains constant.
See:
Data Link - Core Concepts - De-encapsulation (router behavior)
Internet Layer → Basics → IP Addressing → Subnetting & Gateways → Default Gateway & Routing Tables
### Viewing MAC Addresses
You can view MAC addresses using operating system tools: #### Windows ```shell ipconfig /all ``` Displays network interface details and the MAC address (Physical Address).
  • Network interface details
  • MAC address (Physical Address)
#### Linux / Ubuntu ```shell ip link show ``` ```shell ifconfig -a ``` For more information on ipconfig:
See: Internet Layer → Basics → ICMP & Commands → ipconfig (Viewing & Renewing IP Configuration)
MAC addresses appear as:
  • link/ether
#### macOS ```shell ifconfig ``` MAC addresses appear as:
  • ether
### MAC Address vs IP Address
  • MAC address, local delivery (Layer 2)
  • IP address, network delivery (Layer 3)
Both are required for complete communication across networks.
### Summary
MAC addressing enables local device identification. Without MAC addresses, switches would have no way to forward frames within a network.

Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/NetworkAcce.title: Bandwidth
Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/NetworkAcce.bodyMd:
# Bandwidth --- ## What is Bandwidth? --- Bandwidth is the amount of data that can move through a communication medium over a specific period of time — usually measured in bits per second. Bandwidth represents the theoretical maximum capacity of a link under ideal conditions. A higher bandwidth means more data can be transmitted at once, which is essential for things like: - Streaming video - Online gaming - Cloud-based applications Different physical media (like copper, fiber, or wireless) support different transmission rates. Bandwidth is affected by: - The properties of the medium (e.g., type of cable) - Technology being used - Physical laws (like signal interference and attenuation) --- ## Units of Bandwidth --- Bandwidth is measured in bits per second (bps) and its larger units. The tables below outline the most common units used in networking:
Unit of Bandwidth Abbreviation
Bits per second bps
Kilobits per second Kbps
Megabits per second Mbps
Gigabits per second Gbps
Terabits per second Tbps

Abbreviation Equivalence
bps 1 bps = fundamental unit of bandwidth
Kbps 1 Kbps = 1,000 bps = 10³ bps
Mbps 1 Mbps = 1,000,000 bps = 10⁶ bps
Gbps 1 Gbps = 1,000,000,000 bps = 10⁹ bps
Tbps 1 Tbps = 1,000,000,000,000 bps = 10¹² bps
--- ## Key Takeaways --- - Bandwidth measures how much data can flow through a connection per second. - It is different from latency, which measures how quickly a single bit of data travels. - Common units like Mbps or Gbps help estimate the speed and efficiency of a connection. - Physical media, technology, and environmental factors all affect available bandwidth. --- ## 📎 Related Topics ---
See: Switching → Hardware & Port Types → Ethernet Speed History
See: TCP/IP Model → Network Access → Physical → Data Transmission Methods
---

Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/NetworkAcce.title: Throughput
Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/NetworkAcce.bodyMd:
# Throughput --- ## What is Throughput? --- Throughput is the measure of the **actual amount of data** transferred over a network connection during a given period of time. It is similar to bandwidth but accounts for **real-world performance**, not just theoretical capacity. Unlike bandwidth, throughput is often **lower than the maximum bandwidth** due to various influencing factors, such as: - The amount of data being sent and received - The types of data being transmitted - The number of devices and latency between source and destination Latency refers to the time, including delays, it takes for data to travel from one point to another. --- ### Throughput vs Bandwidth --- While **bandwidth** is the maximum potential capacity of a connection, **throughput** is what you actually get in real-world performance. - Bandwidth describes the size of the “pipe.” - Throughput describes how much data successfully flows through that pipe. Throughput measures the total amount of data transferred, including: - Useful application content - Background network traffic For example: - Control messages - Error corrections - Retransmitted packets All count toward throughput. This means a high-bandwidth network may still experience poor throughput if congestion, delays, or errors are present. --- ### Practical Limitations --- In networks with multiple segments or paths, throughput is ultimately limited by the **slowest segment** — the weakest link in the chain. Even if most parts of the route support high bandwidth, a single lower-capacity segment can reduce overall end-to-end throughput. --- ### Measuring Throughput --- Throughput is commonly measured in **megabits per second (Mbps)** or **gigabits per second (Gbps)** — the same units used for bandwidth. A common real-world tool to check network throughput is: - speedtest.net It measures both: - Download speed — how fast data is received - Upload speed — how fast data is sent Most home internet services provide faster download speeds than upload speeds. --- ## 📎 Related Topics ---
See: Physical - Bandwidth & Throughput - Bandwidth
See: Physical - Transmission Concepts - Data Transmission Methods
---

Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/NetworkAcce.title: CoaxialCable
Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/NetworkAcce.bodyMd:
# Introduction --- ### Coaxial Cable
Coaxial cable is a copper-based transmission medium commonly used for cable television, broadband internet (DOCSIS), and satellite communication systems. Unlike twisted-pair Ethernet cabling, coaxial cable uses a single solid copper core to carry signals.
### Coaxial Cable Structure
A coaxial cable consists of:
Component Purpose
Copper Core Carries the electrical signal
Dielectric Insulation Separates core from shielding
Braided / Foil Shield Protects against electromagnetic interference (EMI)
Outer Jacket Physical protection
The shielding allows coaxial cable to carry high-frequency broadband signals with reduced interference.
### Common Coaxial Connectors
Common connector types include: - F-Type – Common in cable TV and cable internet installations - BNC – Used in legacy networking and some specialized equipment These connectors attach to the solid copper core and shielding to complete the signal path.
### Where Coax Is Used Today
Coaxial cable is commonly used for: - Cable television systems - Cable broadband (DOCSIS) - Satellite communication systems - Some legacy Ethernet deployments In modern home networks, coax typically terminates at a cable modem, which converts the signal into Ethernet for LAN use.

Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/NetworkAcce.title: CopperConnectors
Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/NetworkAcce.bodyMd:
# Introduction --- ### Copper Ethernet Connectors --- Copper Ethernet cables use a standardized connector commonly referred to as: - RJ45 - 8P8C (8-position, 8-contact) RJ45 is the common physical connector used for twisted-pair Ethernet cabling. --- ### Plug vs Jack ---
Component Description
Plug The male connector crimped onto the end of a patch cable
Jack The female connector found on switches, routers, wall plates, and patch panels
Plugs insert into jacks to complete the connection. --- ### Where RJ45 Connectors Are Used --- RJ45 connectors are commonly found on: - Switch Ethernet ports - Router LAN ports - Wall outlets - Patch panels - Desktop network interface cards (NICs) --- ### Termination Standards --- Ethernet twisted pair cabling is terminated using wiring standards such as: - T568A - T568B These standards define the order of the wire pairs inside the connector.
See: Physical → Wiring Standards → T568A vs T568B
--- ### Keystone Jacks and Patch Panels --- Structured cabling installations often use: - Keystone jacks (in wall plates) - Patch panels (in racks) These allow permanent cabling to terminate cleanly and connect to switches using patch cables. --- ### Electrical Signaling --- Copper Ethernet uses: - Electrical voltage changes - Balanced signaling across twisted pairs Because copper carries electrical current, it is: - Susceptible to electromagnetic interference (EMI) - Limited by signal attenuation over distance
Compare With: Fiber Connectors & Transceivers
---

Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/NetworkAcce.title: EthernetCablingCategories
Networking/TCPIPModel/Layers/NetworkAcce.bodyMd:
# Ethernet Twisted Pair Cabling --- Most wired Ethernet networks use twisted-pair copper cabling. Because Ethernet is the foundation of most local area networks (LANs), twisted-pair cabling is the most commonly deployed network medium. This cabling is categorized using a Category (Cat) rating, which defines performance characteristics such as: - Supported signaling frequency - Crosstalk tolerance - Distance limitations - Typical Ethernet speeds Category ratings define electrical performance standards — not the Ethernet protocol itself.
## Cable Construction
### Why the Wires Are Twisted
Ethernet twisted-pair cable contains: - 4 wire pairs - Each pair twisted at specific intervals Twisting reduces: - Electromagnetic interference (EMI) - Crosstalk between pairs - Signal distortion This improves reliability over distance.
### UTP vs STP
Type Description
UTP (Unshielded Twisted Pair) Most common in homes and offices; no additional shielding
STP (Shielded Twisted Pair) Includes shielding to reduce EMI in electrically noisy environments
Most residential installations use UTP.
## Common Cable Categories
Category Common Use
Cat5e Reliable for 1 Gbps at standard Ethernet distances
Cat6 Improved crosstalk performance; common in modern installs
Cat6a Better performance at higher frequencies; used for higher-speed structured cabling
Cat8 High-speed short-run copper used in specialized environments
Cable category influences maximum reliable speed over standard Ethernet distances (commonly up to 100 meters).
## Patch Cables vs Structured Cabling
A patch cable is a short, pre-terminated Ethernet cable used to connect devices. Examples: - PC → Switch - Switch → Router - Switch → Patch panel
Definition: See Glossary → Patch Cable
In contrast, structured cabling refers to permanent in-wall installations terminated at: - Wall jacks - Patch panels
### When Wired Connections Are Preferred
Even in wireless homes, wired Ethernet is commonly used for: - Gaming consoles - Smart TVs - Desktop PCs - Network storage devices Wired links provide: - Lower latency - More consistent throughput - Reduced wireless congestion ---
